Tafseer:
'Subhan' is a word of glorification: 'Subhan', as has been clarified in various places, is a word of glorification and declaring perfection. That is, Allah's essence is pure and free from every defect and flaw. The discourse is initiated with this word on occasions where the purpose is to remove some ill-suspicion or misunderstanding regarding God. Here, the introduction to the event of the Night Journey (Isra' / Mi'raj) is raised with this word because this event was also destined to remove a very major ill-suspicion of the Jews and the polytheists regarding God. Both of these groups were in possession of the two greatest centers of God's religion, and contrary to their fundamental purpose of construction, they had turned them not only into hubs of polytheism and idol-worship but also—as Jesus said, 'You have made my Father's house a den of thieves'—they had turned them into a den of thieves and betrayers. Both of these sacred Houses were entirely in the possession of betrayers and dishonest people. And they were acting in them according to their own whims as if the actual owner of these Houses was sleeping with oil in his ears and a bandage over his eyes, and would never wake up to take account of them. The event of the Night Journey, as we indicated earlier, was the introduction to the fact that now the trust of these Houses was about to be handed over to the one who would fulfill their original purpose of construction. For this reason, He initiated its description with the word 'Subhan', and at the end of the verse, by referring to His attributes 'indeed, He is the Hearing, the Seeing', He made it clear that those foolish ones who assumed God to be blind and deaf should now open their ears and eyes. Now the time of their judgment has arrived. The actual Hearing and Seeing is God alone, and now He will judge people in the light of His perfect knowledge and awareness.

'Abd' Refers to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): 'He took His servant by night.' 'Isra' means to travel by night, and when it becomes transitive through 'bi', it means to take someone somewhere by night. Although the meaning of setting out or taking someone by night is already inherent in its concept, in common usage this word is sometimes divested of this meaning; for this reason, the restriction of 'by night' (laylan) is intended to emphasize that this event occurred during the night itself. In 'bi-abdihi', 'servant' refers to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). The use of this word for the Prophet on this occasion is proof of his extreme closeness to Allah, Allah's extreme love for him, and his perfect state of servanthood. It is as if his personality is not in need of any other praise or introduction. The word 'servant' (abd) itself points out and distinguishes him from the entire creation as the true referent of this word.

The Meaning of Al-Masjid-al-Haram and Al-Masjid-al-Aqsa: '...from the Sacred Mosque to the Farthest Mosque, whose surroundings We have blessed.' By the Sacred Mosque, it is obvious that the Kaaba is meant. As for the other mosque, its introduction is made through two attributes: one is 'Aqsa' (the Farthest), and the second is 'whose surroundings We have blessed'. 'Aqsa' means the one situated far away. This mosque was in Jerusalem, at a distance of about 40 days' journey for the inhabitants of Makkah, who are the primary addressees of this discourse; for this reason, He described it with the attribute 'Aqsa' so that the mind could easily transition to it. Then, by attaching the attribute 'whose surroundings We have blessed', He also pointed to the land in which this mosque is situated. This is an expression of both the spiritual and material fertility of this land. As for its status in terms of spiritual blessings, it is sufficient to consider how many prophets had the honor of having this land as their birthplace and burial place—an honor not obtained by any other region.

The Purpose of the Night Journey: '...that We might show him of Our signs.' This explains the purpose of this journey—that Allah willed to show His servant some of His signs. Keep in view the eloquence of the style of expression: the preceding matter was described in the third person (absentee pronoun), which is proof of the grandeur of His status, and here the first-person plural pronoun (mutakallim) has come, which manifests a specific, direct attention. He stated: We made this journey occur so that We might show Our servant some of Our signs. What these signs were is not mentioned here, but the context is proof that it refers to those monuments, observations, lights, and blessings with which both of these Houses were filled. The purpose of showing them is obviously that Allah's will should become clear to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—that now this entire trust is about to be snatched from the ungrateful and covenant-breakers to be handed over to you. It is as if in this extremely difficult period of the call, the glad tidings of Allah's support and victory being given to you received a further seal of confirmation through this journey of the Night Journey, and what was about to happen was also shown to you.

As to the question of whether what was shown to you was shown in a vision (ru'ya) or in wakefulness, the Qur'an itself has answered this question ahead in this very Surah. He stated: 'And We did not make the vision which We showed you except as a trial for the people' (Al-Isra': 60). Clearly, the 'vision' (ru'ya) referred to here cannot mean any other vision except the one referred to in the verse under discussion with the words 'that We might show him of Our signs'. The word 'ara'at' (showing) has come in several places in the Qur'an to mean showing in a vision, and the commentators have also taken it to mean this very vision. For this reason, its being a vision is clear in its place and accepted; but it must be remembered that taking 'ru'ya' in the sense of a common dream is by no means correct. Dreams can be disorganized dreams, but the vision shown to prophets (peace be upon them) is a true vision (ru'ya-e-sadiqah). It has several distinguishing aspects that must be kept in mind.

The Distinguishing Aspects of a True Vision (Ru'ya-e-Sadiqah): The first thing is that a true vision is one of the mediums of divine revelation. Just as Allah sends down His revelation upon His prophets and messengers through an angel in the form of speech, He similarly guides them sometimes in the form of a vision. The second thing is that this vision is highly clear, unambiguous, and bright 'like the break of dawn', regarding which the prophet possesses complete clarity of mind and peace of heart. If there is anything in it of a metaphorical nature, Allah also clarifies its interpretation to His prophet. The third thing is that where it is intended to make one observe events and realities, this medium is more satisfying for the prophet because in this way, the complete details of the events are observed, and those meanings and realities also become personified before him which can hardly be captured in words. The fourth thing is that the observation of a vision is more definitive, wider, and thousands of times deeper and far-reaching than the observation of the physical eye. The eye can suffer from illusion, but a true vision is free from illusion; the eye can see only within a limited circle, but a vision encompasses a highly wide circle at once; the eye is unable to observe realities and meanings, its reach being limited to physical objects. But a vision captures meanings, realities, lights, and manifestations as well. Moses wanted to see the divine manifestation with his physical eyes, but he could not bear its impact. On the contrary, our Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was shown various observations on the Night of Journey, all of which he made, and under no circumstances did his gaze waver.

The context and place of 'indeed, He is the Hearing, the Seeing' has been clarified above. It contains an aspect of restriction. It is to express the reality that the actual Hearing and Seeing is God alone. Nothing is hidden from His hearing and seeing; if others have received any share in it, it is granted by God and is highly limited. The purpose of referring to these attributes, as we indicated above, is to warn both the polytheists of the Quraysh and the Children of Israel: do not assume God to be unaware of your deeds; He is hearing and seeing everything.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 2 to 8: Strike on the Pride and Arrogance of the Jews: In the subsequent verses, a blow is struck against the pride of the Jews—that they are the children of Israel and the beloved of God, because of which religious leadership is their monopoly. Due to this pride of theirs, the Jews were not ready to accept anyone's prophethood and messengership outside of their own family; for this reason, they were severe opponents of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) from the very beginning. Then, when he described the observations of the Night Journey and they came to their knowledge, their anger rose even higher, thinking: 'Look, this person is also claiming the inheritance of our prophets and our leadership.' The Qur'an, to take this air out of their heads, clarified that the right to receive God's grace and mercy is not obtained by any nation on the basis of lineage and descent, but is obtained on the basis of faith and righteous deeds. And upon this, He presented the testimony of their own history—showing that it is written in your own scriptures that you will commit rebellion on a major scale twice and spread corruption in the land, and both times God will-force His severe-grip servants upon you who will crush you. Consequently, these prophecies were fulfilled letter by letter. Whenever you reformed, God showed mercy to you; and whenever you spread corruption, God rained down the whips of His punishment upon you. This very stage is before you at this time too. Our Messenger's call has opened the path of reform and success for you. If you accept this call, you will become equal sharers in its blessings; and if you reject this call, remember that We have not gone anywhere. We will take account of you again in the same way We took account of you before. Read the verses:

'And We gave Moses the Scripture and made it a guidance for the Children of Israel that you not take other than Me as a defender.'

'Wakeel' Means Defender and Trustee: 'Scripture' refers to the Torah, and 'wakeel' means defender, trustee, and that Being upon Whom one places complete trust to hand over all his matters to Him.

The History of the Jews' Corruption: This verse is an introduction to the description of the Jews' corruption and mischief which is coming in the subsequent verses. It is stated that We gave Moses the Scripture and made it a guidance for the Children of Israel, in which this clear instruction was written: do not take anyone as a defender besides Me. The purpose of referring to this is to clarify that they did not appreciate this arrangement of guidance from Allah. They cast His scripture behind their backs, and despite explicit instructions to avoid polytheism, they indulged in the impurities and pollutions of polytheism.

The Teaching of Monotheism in the Torah: Referring to the teaching of monotheism after mentioning the Scripture is an indication that this matter stands as the center of gravity for all religious teachings. On this very basis is the foundation of the entire shariah, and only as long as a group remains attached to it does it remain attached to the religion. Wherever the attachment to this center of gravity became weak, the entire religion was gradually ruined.

It is needless to explain that the Torah is full of the teaching of monotheism. To avoid length, we content ourselves with quoting only one reference. In Exodus 20:2: 'I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery. You shall have no other gods before me. You shall not make for yourself a carved image, or any likeness of anything that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth. You shall not bow down to them or serve them, for I the Lord your God am a jealous God...'

How much compatibility exists between the Qur'an's words 'that you not take other than Me as a defender' and the Torah's words 'You shall have no other gods before me'! Yet, despite these clear instructions, the Jews fell into polytheism and idol-worship repeatedly, over which their prophets lamented in highly painful words and reproached them. Jesus went to the extent of telling the Jews: 'You are the one who committed adultery on the very first night.'

The vocative particle is omitted here. Meaning: along with this teaching of monotheism, they were also reminded: keep always in mind that you are from the offspring of those righteous ones whom Allah saved along with Noah in their ship. Noah was a grateful servant of Allah; so you too, like him, remain grateful to God and established on His monotheism, otherwise remember that just as Allah cleansed His earth of the people of Noah as a punishment for their polytheism, He will similarly cleanse His earth of your existence too.

'And We decreed for the Children of Israel in the Scripture: 'You will surely cause corruption on the earth twice and you will surely reach [a degree of] great haughtiness.'

A Style of Arabic: The preposition 'ila' (to) after 'qadayna' (We decreed) is proof according to the rule of Arabic that 'ablagna' (We conveyed) or a similar word is omitted here. Meaning: We decided and informed the Children of Israel of Our decision.

The Two Major Corruptions of the Jews: 'In the Scripture' (fil-Kitab) refers to all the scriptures of the Jews. In the Qur'an, this word has come to refer to both the Torah and the scriptures of the other prophets of the Children of Israel. In the verse, of the two major corruptions of the Jews and their consequences, the first corruption and its warning end were informed of by David, Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel (peace be upon them); and the second corruption and its consequences were warned of by Jesus. 'Corruption' (fasad) refers to rebellion against God's monotheism and His shariah. Although the history of the Jews is full of such corruptions—consequently, details of one corruption have passed under the event of the snatching of the Ark of the Covenant in Surah Al-Baqarah—the corruptions referred to here are those whose consequences left the entire nation of the Jews humiliated and crushed. The words in which David prophesied the first corruption and its end are these:

'They did not destroy the peoples, as the Lord commanded them, but they mixed with the nations and learned to do as they did. They served their idols, which became a snare to them. They sacrificed their sons and their daughters to the demons; they poured out innocent blood, the blood of their sons and daughters... Therefore the anger of the Lord was kindled against his people, and he abhorred his heritage; he gave them into the hand of the nations, so that those who hated them ruled over them.' (Psalms 106:34-41).

Regarding the prophecy of the second corruption, the words of Jesus are these: 'Truly, I say to you, there will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down.' (Matthew 24:2).

In Luke: 'Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me, but weep for yourselves and for your children. For behold, the days are coming when they will say, 'Blessed are the barren and the wombs that never bore and the breasts that never nursed!' Then they will begin to say to the mountains, 'Fall on us,' and to the hills, 'Cover us.''

At the end of the verse, the words 'So We will punish you twice' or similar words are omitted. Meaning: We had informed the Children of Israel of Our decision in the Scripture that you will surely cause corruption on the earth twice and reach a degree of great haughtiness, and We will punish you severely both times. Since this matter was completely obvious and its complete details were coming in the subsequent verses, He omitted it here.

'So when the [time of] promise came for the first of them, We sent against you servants of Ours - those of military prowess, and they probed [even] within the homes, and it was a promise fulfilled.'

'Iza' (When) does not come solely for the future, but also comes for describing a habit or way, and sometimes for depicting a scene. Here, it is for depicting a scene. We have kept this in view in the translation. The meaning is: see, when the time of the first promise arrives, We force against you Our powerful servants to make you taste Our punishment; they enter even within your homes, and God's definitive promise is fulfilled. The verb 'ba'atha' (sent), when it comes with the preposition 'ala' (against), implies both urging and forcing against someone.

Depicting the Humiliation and Disgrace of the Jews: 'and they probed [even] within the homes'—this is a depiction of the extreme humiliation and disgrace of the Jews, because when the enemy is so powerful that he enters within the homes, it means he has despoiled honor and dignity entirely. Here, He contented Himself with mentioning only this because it was sufficient for depicting the humiliation; but ahead, reference will also come to the fact that this enemy did not content himself with entering the homes, but also completely desecrated the Sanctuary of Al-Aqsa.

Nebuchadnezzar's Attack and the Jews' Captivity: This points to the attack of Nebuchadnezzar, the king of Babylon and Nineveh, who turned Jerusalem upside down in 586 BC. Jeremiah the prophet had prophesied it thus:

'Therefore thus says the Lord of hosts: Because you have not heard my words, behold, I will send for all the tribes of the north, declares the Lord, and for Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon, my servant, and I will bring them against this land...'

Hear the further details of their warning: 'And I will banish from them the voice of mirth and the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride, the grinding of the millstones and the light of the lamp. This whole land shall become a ruin and a waste, and these nations shall serve the king of Babylon seventy years.' (Jeremiah 25:8-11).

Hear the lament of Jeremiah: 'How the Lord in his anger has set the daughter of Zion under a cloud! He has cast down from heaven to earth the splendor of Israel; he has not remembered his footstool in the day of his anger. The Lord has swallowed up without mercy all the habitations of Jacob; in his wrath he has thrown down the strongholds of the daughter of Judah; he has brought down to the ground in dishonor the kingdom and its rulers. He has cut down in fierce anger all the might of Israel...' (Lamentations of Jeremiah 2:1-3).

In the verse, Nebuchadnezzar and his forces are referred to with the words 'servants of Ours - those of military prowess'. This is not used in terms of their religion and piety, but solely because they acted as the instrument and medium for executing and implementing God's will. Although they were corrupt themselves, they implemented His will in cleansing a very major heap of corruption, due to which they received a relationship with God. The Children of Israel possessed the delusion: 'We are the children of Allah and His beloved.' God made it clear to them that those shoes with which you are beaten carry some value in God's eyes, but you carry no value.

'Then We gave back to you a return of victory over them. And We aided you with wealth and sons and made you more numerous in manpower.' After a period of captivity and misery, a desire for reform arose within the Children of Israel, so Allah also turned toward them, blessed their wealth and offspring, and His support appeared for them in the form of Cyrus, the King of Persia, defeating the Chaldeans in 539 BC and taking control of their land. He freed the Jews from captivity and allowed them to return to their homeland to rebuild it, after which the Children of Israel attained a major revival.

'[And We said], If you do good, you do good for yourselves; and if you do evil, [you do it] to yourselves.' Then when the final promise came, [We sent your enemies] to sadden your faces and to enter the temple [i.e., the Masjid in Jerusalem] as they entered it the first time, and to destroy what they overtook with utter destruction.'

The Second Destruction of the Jews at the Hands of Titus: 'If you do good...'—meaning, inherent in this salvation obtained after a long captivity was this lesson: now, if you adopt the path of goodness, you will bring benefit to yourselves; and if you adopt the path of rebellion again, you will face its consequence in the same manner. This writing on the wall was present and your prophets had informed you of it, but what happened was what had been prophesied—that you fell into the same corruption again. Consequently, when the second term for your punishment arrived, We forced against you Our other powerful servants to make your faces look miserable, to enter the temple just as the first ones had entered it, and to completely destroy everything they overtook. In 'to sadden your faces' (li-yasoo'oo wujoohakum), the words 'We sent against you servants of Ours - those of military prowess' are omitted. Since its context was clear, He omitted it, and the 'lam' in 'li-yasoo'oo' points to it.

From the study of Bible history, it is known that this points to the destruction that befell the Jews in 70 AD at the hands of the Roman Emperor Titus, to which Jesus had pointed.

'[And We said], 'It may be that your Lord will have mercy upon you. But if you return [to sin], We will return [to punishment]. And We have made Hell for the disbelievers a prison-bed.'

'Haseer' means a prison-bed or an enclosure in which animals are kept.

Salvation for the Jews in the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): This is an address to those Jews who were present at the time of the revelation of these verses, and were supporting and backing the polytheists of the Quraysh in opposing the Qur'an. Addressing them, it is said: what has happened in the past has been narrated to you. Now, if you want your well-being, adopt the call of this unlettered Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) which has opened the path of salvation for you, and reform your future. If you adopt the path of repentance and reform, God will show mercy to you; and if you act in the same manner as you did before, We will take account of you in the same way We took account of you before. And also remember that the humiliation and disgrace you face in this world is in its place, and ahead of you is the prison-bed of Hell in which all of you will be thrown.

The tone of this verse is highly remarkable. First, He spoke in the third person. Then, in 'if you return, We will return', the first-person plural (mutakallim) came. In the first part, there is an attitude of disregard—meaning that if you adopt the correct path, you will bring benefit to yourselves, and if you do not, you will invite your own doom, doing no harm to God. In the second part, there is a highly severe threat; for this reason, the first-person plural came, which is more appropriate for threats, and it also contains ambiguity and omission. It is stated that We will return, but it is not stated in what form We will return. This is left to the understanding of the intelligent, and the entire severity of this phrase is inherent in this ambiguity.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 9 to 21: In the subsequent verses, both the polytheists of the Quraysh and the Jews are invited to believe in this Qur'an which is calling to the same straight path of human nature to which all the prophets called. The end of those who believe in this call and those who deny it has been clarified, and those who demand signs of punishment while closing their eyes to the signs spread in the cosmos and the clear verses of the Qur'an have been reproached. Under this, the divine sunnah regarding the punishment of nations has been clarified in brief. Read the verses:

Commentary on Verses 9 and 10: 'Indeed, this Quran guides to that which is most suitable and gives good tidings to the believers who do righteous deeds that they will have a great reward. And that those who do not believe in the Hereafter - We have prepared for them a painful punishment.'

The Meaning of 'Most Suitable' (Aqwam): 'Aqwam' means straight and upright—that is, the path which is in complete harmony with human nature and intellect, and which leads to God.

Inviting the Jews and the Polytheists to Believe in the Qur'an: This is an invitation to both the Children of Israel and the polytheists of the Quraysh to believe in the Qur'an—showing that if you want to reach God, adopt this path instead of wandering in crooked ways. It gives glad tidings of a great reward to those who believe and live a life of righteous deeds. As for those who do not believe in the Hereafter and are rejecting this Qur'an because of it, there is a painful punishment for them.

'And man supplicates for evil as he supplicates for good, and man is ever hasty.'

This describes the state of those opponents of the Qur'an who were demanding a sign of punishment instead of believing in it. Since this demand was highly foolish and highly destructive for themselves, the statement is put in general terms with an attitude of regret, turning away from them: man's state is strange—with the same zeal with which he should seek goodness, he demands calamity and destruction for himself. Instead of benefiting from this respite to reform his life, he wants to see that punishment as soon as possible of which he is being warned.

'And We have made the night and the day two signs, and We erased the sign of the night and made the sign of the day visible that you may seek bounty from your Lord and that you may know the number of years and the account [of time]. And everything We have set out in detail.'

In the first part of this verse, after 'and We erased the sign of the night' (fa-mahawna ayatal-layl), the words 'making it dark so that you may rest' or similar words are omitted, which are clarified by the subsequent words 'made the sign of the day visible that you may seek bounty from your Lord'. That is, We made the night dark so that you may obtain rest in it, and made the day bright so that you may seek God's provision and favor in it.

Cosmic Signs for Those Who Demand a Sign of Punishment: Those who demand a sign of punishment are drawn to the signs of the cosmos—showing that if a sign is desired, why is it necessary that it be a sign of punishment? After all, why do they not look at this night and the coming of the day after it—is this any less of a sign? The night comes and stretches the bed of comfort and security for you, in which you rest after the day's fatigue to become fresh again; this is why God has made it dark and peaceful. Then the day comes, in which you engage fresh in your economic activities and the search for God's provision; this is why your Lord has made it bright.

'and that you may know the number of years and the account': A further benefit of this disciplined coming and going of day and night is stated—showing that in this way, you also know the calculation of months and years, and other calculations too. If this difference of day and night did not exist, what would you have made the sign and mark of distinction for determining anything?

'And everything We have set out in detail'—meaning, besides these signs of the cosmos, We have also bestowed this favor upon you that We have explained every necessary thing in this Book of Ours, so that this Book itself may be sufficient for the satisfaction of the one who reflects.

It must be remembered here that the Qur'an has not contented itself with pointing to the reality of day and night in this manner, but has clarified its further aspects in other places. For example: from the compatibility between them despite their contrast, He has argued for monotheism; from the coming of morning after the night, He has drawn attention to the Resurrection. The details of these things have passed before in this book, and their further details will come ahead.

Commentary on Verses 13 and 14: 'And [for] every person We have fastened his fate to his neck, and We will produce for him on the Day of Resurrection a record which he will encounter spread open. [It will be said], 'Read your record. Sufficient is yourself against you this Day as accountant.'

The Meaning of 'Fate' (Ta'irah): 'Ta'ir' literally means a bird, but since the Arabs used birds for drawing omens and, according to their assumption, for knowing fate, this word began to be used in the sense of fate, share, and destiny. This is a warning to those who show haste for the punishment—showing that if they are showing this haste relying on their assumed partners and intercessors, they should keep in mind that We have hung every person's fate to his neck. Whatever anyone did, its complete details will be present before him in the form of an open register, and We will say to him: 'Read your record; you are sufficient to take your own account; there is no need for any other's interference in it.' The meaning is: on the Day of Reckoning, no one will carry another's burden. Everyone's deeds will be before him, and everyone will have to carry his own burden.

'Whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] himself, and whoever goes astray only goes astray against it. And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And never would We punish until We sent a messenger.'

Sending a Messenger for the Completion of Proof: This is a further explanation of the above subject—showing that whoever adopts the path of guidance does so for his own benefit, and whoever walks on the path of misguidance will face its consequence himself. No other soul will carry another's burden. 'And never would We punish until We sent a messenger' points to the divine sunnah that Allah has always maintained regarding the punishment of nations: He sends His messengers to fully establish the proof before sending punishment. The meaning is: what haste are you making for the punishment—one stage of it has already been passed with the coming of God's Messenger to you. Now, only as much delay remains for the punishment to come as is required for the proof to be fully established against you. It will be your doom if you do not benefit from this opportunity.

'And when We intend to destroy a city, We command its affluent, but they defiantly disobey therein; so the word [i.e., decree] becomes deservedly binding upon it, and We destroy it with [complete] destruction.'

The Meaning of 'Command' (Amara): 'Amara' does not come solely in the sense of giving a command, but also comes sometimes in the sense of leaving someone to his state and granting him respite. When you become fed up after trying to make someone understand, you say: 'Do what you want' (Do whatever comes to your mind). Outwardly, the imperative form is used, but its meaning is granting respite. Similarly, Allah also leaves rebellious people to their state after fully establishing His argument against them, so that they may fill their cup of deeds. 'Mutrafeen' refers to the wealthy, affluent class of a nation. Since the reins of the nation are in their hands, the divine sunnah has been that the prophets (peace be upon them) addressed this class first in their call for reform. Then, when this class disappointed them through their stubbornness and sought to kill them, the Prophet migrated, and the nation was seized by divine punishment. This is a further explanation of the divine sunnah regarding punishment. It is stated that when We decide to destroy a town, We leave its wealthy ones to their state, so they defiantly disobey God and commit excesses in it until the proof is fully established against them and their cup of deeds is full. Then God seizes them and destroys that town completely.

'And how many have We destroyed of generations after Noah. And sufficient is your Lord, concerning the sins of His servants, as Acquainted and Seeing.'

In support of the aforementioned divine sunnah, this first presents a reference to history—showing that We have destroyed many generations after Noah. If these people have eyes to see, let them learn a lesson from their conditions. Then, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is comforted: He is well aware of the crimes of the rebellious of your nation and is watching all conditions. When the time comes, He will not delay in deciding their fate either.

'Whoever should desire the immediate - We hasten for him from it what We will to whom We intend. Then We have made for him Hell, which he will [enter to] burn, censured and banished.'

'Aajilah' is the contrasting word of the Hereafter—meaning, this world and its immediate benefits.

The Divine Sunnah Regarding Respite: This describes the divine sunnah regarding respite—showing that those who completely disregard the Hereafter to seek only this world and its immediate benefits are not deprived by God; rather, He grants of it to whom He wills and as much as He wills. However, they will have no share in the Hereafter. Their share in the Hereafter will be only Hell, which they will enter censured and banished.

In this verse, the words 'what We will to whom We intend' must be kept in mind—meaning that this matter too is not in the hands of the world-seekers themselves, such that anyone can obtain as much as he wants; rather, this matter belongs entirely to God's control. He alone grants to whom He wills.

'But whoever desires the Hereafter and exerts for it its [due] exertion while he is a believer - those are the ones whose exertion is appreciated.'

The Truly Successful Group: This describes the seekers of the Hereafter and the truly successful group. It is stated that those who seek the Hereafter, exert the effort due for it, and their breasts are illuminated with faith—indeed, these are the ones whose effort will be accepted by God. The meaning is: such people also receive what is destined for them in this world, and the Hereafter is indeed the home of their success.

In this verse too, the words 'and exerts for it its [due] exertion while he is a believer' must be kept in mind—meaning that the Hereafter will not be obtained merely by wishing for it; rather, the effort due for it is required, alongside faith clean of all pollutions of polytheism. As long as these two things do not accompany the desire for the Hereafter, this wish will remain unfulfilled.

'To each [category] We extend - to these and to those - from the gift of your Lord. And never has the gift of your Lord been restricted.'

God's Gift is General: When the word 'kullan' (each/all) comes after the mention of different groups in this manner, it stands in the place of a definite noun. That is, it refers to those groups which have been mentioned before. The meaning is: the door of your Lord's gift and bounty is not closed to either of the two groups mentioned. Those who, disregardful of the Hereafter, are active day and night in seeking only this world, God provides for them also in this world according to what He has destined for them. He does not deprive them of the world as a punishment for their crime of forgetting Him and the Hereafter. Similarly, those who seek the Hereafter, God provides for them their destined share in this world; He does not deprive them of the world due to their disregard for it, but rather grants them their share in this world too, and in the Hereafter, they will receive the full reward of their effort. The meaning is: when the actual reality is this, why should a person lose the eternal and permanent blessings of the Hereafter for the sake of this fleeting and insignificant world? Why should he not adopt that path by walking on which he becomes deserving of the eternal blessings of the Hereafter, while also enjoying what is destined for him in this world?

'Look how We have favored [in provision] some of them over others. But the Hereafter is greater in degrees [of difference] and greater in distinction.'

That is, see, this reality is completely obvious in its place: God alone has granted more to whom He willed and less to whom He willed; it is not in man's own power to obtain as much as he wants. Similarly, in the Hereafter too, all authority will be in God's hand; He alone will grant honor to whom He wills and humiliation to whom He wills; no one else will possess any influence there to affect His decisions. This Hereafter, in terms of its degrees and ranks, is far greater than the degrees and ranks of this world; so whoever wants to make an effort should seek its degrees and ranks, and why should he destroy the Hereafter for the sake of this world?

Subsequent Subject. Verses 22 to 39: Above you saw that in verse 9, by stating 'Indeed, this Quran guides to that which is most suitable...', the speech turned to some other related matters. Now, the speech connects back to its original sequence, and the explanation of that path of guidance leading to God has begun. This very path is the straight path of human nature and intellect, and it brings into existence that life of justice and benevolence which is pleasing to God and upon which is the foundation of a righteous society and a righteous civilization. Here, keep in mind verse 90 of Surah Al-Nahl, in which the foundations of the Qur'an's commands and prohibitions were clarified. Because the subsequent verses stand as the explanation of that brief point: 'Indeed, Allah orders justice and good conduct and giving to relatives and forbids immorality and bad conduct and oppression...' Read the verses:

'Do not make [as equal] with Allah another deity and [thereby] sit down censured and forsaken.'

Singular Address for the Plural: We have clarified in several places that a singular address is sometimes used for a group. In this case, every individual of the group is addressed individually. Here too is the same case. Consequently, in the middle, the plural form has also been used for the address, which throws light on the actual nature of the address. In the middle of this, if any address has occurred to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), it occurred as the leader of the group; it does not stand as a personal address.

The Meaning of 'Taq'uda' (Sit down): 'Taq'uda' is used here in the sense in which incomplete verbs (af'aal naqisah), such as 'takoon' or 'taseer', are used. In Urdu too, 'sit down' (baith raho) is used in this sense. In this case, this word is divested of the literal meaning of sitting.

The Fundamental Clause of the Qur'an's Path of Guidance: This is the fundamental clause of that justice or path of guidance which the Qur'an teaches: do not associate another deity as a partner with Allah. The Creator, Owner, and Provider is God alone; so associating another as a partner in His rights and His divinity is contrary to justice and is a very great wrong. Consequently, on this very basis, polytheism is termed in the Qur'an as a great wrong (zulm-e-azeem).

The Fate of Making Partners to God: 'and [thereby] sit down censured and forsaken'—this describes the fate of making partners to God, and the place of sitting 'the Day of Resurrection' (Yawm-al-Qiyamah) is omitted here. That is, if you associate another as a partner with God in His rights, you will remain on the Day of Resurrection deserving of censure and forsaken. You will be responsible for your own misfortune, and none of your assumed partners and intercessors will stand with you; rather, they too will curse and hate you.

The Greatest Right After God is of the Parents: That is, it is solely God's prerogative to state whether there is any partner in His rights or not, so His decision is that you must worship none but Him alone. After God, the greatest right that can exist is of the parents upon their offspring, because they become the medium of their birth, nurture, and upbringing. Yet, even their right has not been designated by Allah as being partners in His worship; rather, their right is that they be treated with benevolence. 'Ihsanan' (with benevolence) here is a cognate accusative (maf'ool mutlaq) with an omitted verb, meaning: they must be dealt with in the best possible manner. From the context of the speech, it naturally follows that if there were any slight room to associate anyone in worship, parents would have been the most deserving of it; but when God did not associate even them, making them deserving of only benevolence, what can be said of others!

'Whether one or both of them reach old age [while] with you, say not to them [so much as], 'uff,' and do not repel them but speak to them a noble word.'

'Uff' is a word expressing annoyance of the heart, and 'nahr' means to scold and repel. This explains the benevolence commanded above: if parents reach old age in your presence, neither should any annoyance arise in the heart against them, nor should any disrespectful word escape the tongue before them; rather, whenever there is an occasion to speak to them, speak with respect and obedience, comforting and consoling them.

Parents' Right of Benevolence: The reference to reaching old age is made in the verse merely because this is the time when parents are felt as a burden by those who forget the sacrifices and struggles they went through for them in their childhood. Dutiful offspring remember that just as you were once placed in the lap of your parents as a mere lump of flesh, now your parents have been handed over to you as a structure of bones, and it is your duty to return their favor with benevolence. But not everyone remembers this; this is a reminder of this very fact. Otherwise, the actual reality is that parents are deserving of love, respect, and benevolence in every period of life. Note that just as the previous verse mentioned the greatest justice, this verse mentions the greatest benevolence, which stands as the second fundamental teaching in Qur'anic instructions.

Parents' Right of Obedience and Service: 'And lower to them the wing of humility out of mercy and say, 'My Lord, have mercy upon them as they brought me up [when I was] small.'' 'Zull' means obedience and submission. In the metaphor of the 'wing' (janah), there is an allusion that your parents protected you under their wings during your childhood, just as a bird protects its chick under its wings. Its right is that in their old age, you too keep them protected under your wings of obedience and love. Along with this obedience and submission, the condition 'out of mercy' points to its source and motive—showing that this obedience and submission must be based entirely on affection, love, and mercy, allowing no other motive to interfere, because their affection and love can be repaid only through the motive of love and affection. Without this motive, no one can fulfill the parents' right in their old age.

The Right of Praying for Parents: 'and say, 'My Lord, have mercy upon them...'' Along with service and love, He directed to keep making this prayer for them: 'O my Lord, just as they brought me up with affection and love in my childhood, similarly shower Your love and mercy upon them in this old age.' This prayer is also a right of the parents. It contains a reminder of the duty towards parents that is binding on offspring, and it also acts as a motivator for the motive of love required in dealing with parents.

Sincere Love and Complete Dutifulness for Parents: Caring for parents in their old age with the love and service directed by the Qur'an is no simple matter. In this, not only outward obedience is required, but a pure, heartfelt motive of love and deep attachment is also required. Due to the difficulty of this, the Qur'an has also clarified that the actual requirement is sincere love and complete dutifulness. If this exists, God is fully aware of the state of hearts. In its presence, if any minor, accidental shortcoming occurred, its remedy is through repentance and turning to Allah. Those who constantly seek forgiveness from Allah for such shortcomings, Allah will forgive them.

Commentary on Verses 26 and 27: 'And give the relative his right, and [also] the poor and the traveler, and do not spend wastefully. Indeed, the wasteful are brothers of the devils, and ever is Satan to his Lord ungrateful.'

The Right of Relatives, the Poor, and the Traveler: After the command of justice and benevolence, the third fundamental component of Qur'an's positive commands is 'giving to relatives' (eetaa'i zi'l-qurba), which is being described here. He stated: 'give the relative, the poor, and the traveler their rights.' From this, we learn that in the wealth of every wealthy person, there is a right of his relatives, the poor, and the travelers. The use of the word 'right' (haqq) here shows that this is not merely a voluntary moral duty, but is an obligatory right, the payment of which is binding upon the owner of wealth. If anyone does not pay this right, he will stand as a usurper of rights in the sight of Allah. After the relatives, the second category is of the poor (miskeen). A relative becomes deserving of help by virtue of relationship alone, but the poor is made deserving of it by his poverty. If a relative is also poor, his right becomes twofold. The mention of the poor immediately after the relatives shows the status and rank of the poor in Islamic society. After the poor is the traveler (ibn-us-sabeel). It is clear that a traveler becomes deserving of help by virtue of being a traveler alone; it is not necessary for him to be poor, otherwise there was no need to mention him separately. There is no doubt that the state of travel in itself is something that makes a person in need of others' help under normal circumstances, even if he does not fall under the technical definition of the poor.

Instruction to Moderation and Thrift: 'and do not spend wastefully'—obviously, when there are rights of others in the wealth of every wealthy person, he cannot be allowed to spend his wealth extravagantly. The correct conduct for him can only be to spend on his own lawful needs with care, moderation, and thrift, and regarding the remaining wealth, he should consider himself a trustee of other right-holders, paying this trust with great care. A person who is not careful and thrifty in his own needs will be busy satisfying his own desires and will have no opportunity to pay the rights of others.

'Indeed, the wasteful are brothers of the devils...'—He stated that those who spend wastefully are brothers of the devils, and Satan is ungrateful to his Lord. When Allah grants His blessing to people, its natural requirement is that they show gratitude to their Lord and spend it on those tasks which bring His pleasure; but Satan tempts them and makes them spend their wealth on those tasks which take them further away from God and closer to Satan.

'And if you [must] turn away from them [i.e., the needy] awaiting mercy from your Lord which you expect, then speak to them a gentle word.'

This is an instruction for that situation when a person is temporarily not in a position to pay the aforementioned rights. He stated: if your circumstances are such that you must turn away from the needy due to temporary constraint, while expecting that your circumstances will improve in the near future and you will be able to help them, then speak to them with sympathy and kindness, parting with them with a good promise for the future.

'And do not make your hand [as] chained to your neck or extend it completely and [thereby] become blamed and insolvent.'

Binding the hand to the neck is a metaphor for extreme miserliness and stinginess, and extending it completely is a metaphor for extravagance and wastefulness.

To prevent any misunderstanding regarding the prohibition of wastefulness in verses 26-27 above, this clarifies the correct point of moderation: the divine intent is not that a person should become completely miserly and stingy regarding his own needs, but what is required is that he adopt moderation and thrift—neither binding his hands completely nor extending them completely, but spending on his own lawful needs with moderation and paying the rights of others. 'And become blamed and insolvent' (fa-taq'uda maloomam-mahsura)—here 'taq'uda' (become) is used in the same sense as in verse 22. The meaning is: if you extend your hands completely, its ultimate result will be that you will stand blamed regarding the rights of others and will find yourself helpless and unable to pay those rights.

'Indeed, your Lord extends provision for whom He wills and restricts [it]. Indeed, He is ever, of His servants, Acquainted and Seeing.'

Here too, 'for whom He wills' (li-man yashaa') is omitted after 'restricts' (yaqdiru), as it is clear from the context.

The Restriction and Expansion of Provision Depends on God's Will: This verse explains the command in the previous verse: 'And do not make your hand [as] chained to your neck'—showing that the restriction and expansion of provision does not depend on a person's own strategies, but depends entirely on God's wisdom and will. He is fully aware of His servants' states and is their guardian and protector. He, according to His wisdom and will, expands provision for whom He wills and restricts it. The correct conduct for a servant is to spend on his own needs with care and moderation and pay the rights of others, taking no step that deviates from this point of moderation in either direction.

'And do not kill your children for fear of poverty. We provide for them and for you. Indeed, their killing is ever a great sin.'

Prohibition of Killing Offspring: This is based on the point mentioned above: when the actual reality is that the Provider is God alone, on what basis does anyone have the right to destroy another life out of fear of what they will eat? The major reason behind the cruel practice of burying daughters alive in the days of Arab ignorance was their assumption that since a female is not an earning member, why carry the burden of her upbringing. The Qur'an struck at the actual motive of this cruel crime and put an end to this barbarism. The movement running in modern times under the name of birth control, and the new methods being invented daily to implement it—though they do not appear physically brutal—are fundamentally based on the same philosophy and belief that motivated the barbarism of Arab ignorance. Like the ignorant Arabs, the civilized man of modern times has assumed himself to be the provider of others. The Qur'an corrected this misguidance by stating: 'We provide for them and for you.' The wild Arabs understood this reality and reformed themselves, but who can explain this to the educated jinn of this age?

'And do not approach unlawful sexual intercourse. Indeed, it is ever an immorality and is evil as a way.'

The Prohibition of Forbidden Things: With verse 31, the description of basic positive commands (justice, benevolence, giving to relatives) has concluded, and now the description of forbidden things (immorality, vice, rebellion) begins.

Prohibition of Fornication and its Motivators: Among forbidden things, fornication (zina) is taken first because this evil strikes at the root of a healthy society. A healthy society is established on a healthy family. A healthy family, with correct natural, spiritual, and emotional ties, can exist only when the relationship of offspring with parents is established on correct blood, correct lineage, and a pure womb. If this is lost, the family is not a family but a herd of animals unaware of natural and spiritual feelings. A herd of animals can neither establish a healthy society nor lead a healthy civilization.

Due to this destructiveness of fornication, the Qur'an took it first among its prohibitions and prohibited it in words that close the door to fornication and all its motivators and precursors. He stated: 'And do not approach unlawful sexual intercourse'—which means, do not even go near it. 'Do not go near it' means, stay far away from all those matters that motivate, incite, or lead closer to fornication. Here, the matter is stated only as a basic principle, so no details have been given. The details will come in Surah Al-Noor, which is the last Surah of this group. In its commentary, if Allah wills, we will explain from what matters the Qur'an has restricted people purely because they are among the precursors and motivators of fornication.

'Indeed, it is ever an immorality and is evil as a way'—this is the argument for the prohibition of fornication: it is an open immorality and an evil way. An open immorality (fahishah) means that its being evil and immoral does not require any logical debate; rather, this is among the oldest recognized realities of human nature. Ever since man has existed in the world, he has never tolerated unrestricted relations between men and women; rather, there have always been strict restrictions on it, and those who broke these restrictions were never accepted warmly in society. 'And is evil as a way'—meaning, this is a completely deviant path from that straight road (tariq-e-aqwam) to which the Qur'an is calling. Those who walk on this path destroy the foundations of a healthy family, a healthy society, and ultimately a stable government.

'And do not kill the soul which Allah has forbidden [to be killed] except by [legal] right. And whoever is killed unjustly - We have given his heir authority, but let him not exceed limits in [the matter of] taking life. Indeed, he has been supported [by the law].'

Prohibition of Killing and the Temperament of Islamic Law: 'The soul which Allah has forbidden [to be killed]' refers to every soul which Allah has made secure and protected, and which is not legally permitted to be killed; it is not lawful for anyone to kill it except by legal right—that is, only under those conditions when the shariah has commanded its execution. If any person is killed without this legal justification, it is an unjust killing. Under such conditions, He stated: 'We have given his heir authority'—We have given the heirs of the deceased complete authority over the killer: they can choose to take retribution (qisas), they can choose to accept blood money (diyat), or they can choose to forgive. Giving complete authority means that the Islamic government will strictly implement the choice of the heirs. 'But let him not exceed limits in [the matter of] taking life. Indeed, he has been supported [by the law]'—this is an instruction for the heirs of the deceased: since they have the support of the law and the government, it is not lawful for them to exceed limits in executing the killer—such as killing others besides the actual killer, or adopting ways of execution which Allah has forbidden, such as burning with fire or mutilation (muthlah).

From this verse, it also emerges that in Islamic law, the primary plaintiff in matters of murder is not the government, but the heirs of the deceased. The duty of the government is solely to implement the choice of the heirs strictly. The government will stand as the plaintiff only when the deceased has no heirs, or if there are heirs but they show no interest in the matter of the deceased. In modern laws, all authority belongs solely to the government, and the heirs are completely excluded from the matter. In our view, modern laws are devoid of many blessings of Islamic law. We have discussed this issue in another book of ours; there is no room to repeat it here.

'And do not approach the property of an orphan, except in the way that is best, until he reaches maturity. And fulfill [every] commitment. Indeed, the commitment is ever [that about which one will be] questioned.'

Guarding the Orphan's Property: With the same intensity with which He prohibited fornication and its motivators above, He has prohibited here any unauthorized interference in the property of an orphan. He stated: 'except in the way that is best, do not approach the property of an orphan.' This means that only that interference of the guardians in the orphan's property is permitted which is intended for its development, preservation, and growth. Any interference separate from this is a breach of trust, because the orphan's property is in reality a trust in the hands of the guardians. 'Until he reaches maturity'—meaning, this guardianship is only until the orphan reaches his maturity and age of discretion. When he reaches maturity and discretion, he is the master of his property; his property must be handed over to him.

Instruction to Fulfill Commitments: 'And fulfill [every] commitment. Indeed, the commitment is ever [that about which one will be] questioned.' Along with the prohibition of breaching trust in the orphan's property, He prohibited breaching trust in all commitments and covenants—commanding that every commitment made must be fulfilled, and no breach of trust must be committed, because everyone will be questioned about their commitments before Allah. This includes all types of commitments—whether they came into existence in the form of a formal contract, or did not take the form of a contract but are practically and customarily understood as a commitment. Whatever the nature of the commitment, if it is not contrary to the shariah, fulfilling it is necessary. We have clarified under the commentary of Surah Al-Ma'idah that the status of the shariah itself is in reality a covenant between the servants and God. On the fulfillment of these commitments depends the establishment and survival of a healthy family, a healthy society, and a stable government.

'And give full measure when you measure, and weigh with an even balance. That is good and the best outcome.'

Emphasis on Giving Full Measure and Weight: After the emphasis on fulfilling commitments, this is the emphasis on giving full measure and weight—commanding that in your mutual transactions, when you measure or weigh anything, commit no dishonesty in measure or weight. Measure with correct measures, and weigh with correct scales. This way is better and is the best in terms of the outcome. That is, from the economic and commercial perspective too, this way is beneficial, and in terms of the ultimate end too, this way is best, because God likes this way. The nation that makes dishonest weighing its habit—though some of its individuals might assume they are earning profit—actually destroys that foundation of justice and truth upon which the establishment and survival of a healthy society and a healthy civilization depend.

'And do not pursue that of which you have no knowledge. Indeed, the hearing, the sight and the heart - about all those [one] will be questioned.'

'Qafautuhu' or 'qafautu atharahu' means: 'I followed in his footsteps.' 'And do not pursue that of which you have no knowledge'—meaning, do not follow after something about which you do not possess reliable knowledge, and do not repeat any statement about anyone based merely on conjecture and rumor.

Prohibition of Slander, Accusation, etc.: This is the prohibition of all such matters as slander, accusation, and the like. The society that the Qur'an wants to establish is based on good suspicion and trust; for this reason, no such statement must be spoken about anyone which is based merely on conjecture or rumor and is harmful to his honor and reputation. He stated that those who speak such irresponsible words must remember that hearing, sight, and heart—about all of these, one will be questioned one Day.

'And do not walk upon the earth exultantly. Indeed, you will never tear the earth [asunder], and you will never reach the mountains in height.'

Prohibition of Pride and Arrogance: 'Marah' means to walk exultantly and arrogantly. The person who walks exultantly and arrogantly strikes his feet on the earth and walks with his neck raised. This is the gait of the arrogant and proud. He stated: do not adopt this gait of the arrogant and proud. Remember, no matter how much you strike your feet on the earth, you can never tear God's earth asunder; similarly, no matter how much you stretch your chest and raise your neck and head, you can never reach the height of the mountains. The meaning is: seeing the power of God Who has spread this vast earth beneath your feet—before which your status is not even of an insect or an ant—and Who has erected these high mountains before you—before which your status is not even of a squirrel—what is the meaning of acting arrogantly on His earth? Recognize your status and remain always humble before God's majesty and glory.

Keep in view that a person's physical gait reflects his inner state. This is proof that the person who walks arrogantly has no concept of God's majesty and power in his heart. Those in whose hearts the concept of God's majesty and power is established are overcome by humility and meekness. Instead of acting arrogantly, they walk with heads bowed and gentle steps.

'All that - its evil is ever, in the sight of your Lord, detested.'

Pointing to all the aforementioned prohibited matters, He stated that the evil of each of these actions is highly detested in the sight of your Lord. The word 'detested' (makrooh) here is not in its technical jurisprudential meaning, but in its literal meaning. That is, all these matters are highly disliked and abominable to God. From this, it naturally follows that those who commit any of these actions will also become detested in the sight of God.

'That is from what your Lord has revealed to you, [O Muhammad], of wisdom. And do not make [equal] with Allah another deity, lest you be thrown into Hell, blamed and banished.'

These Matters are Components of Wisdom: 'That' (zaalik) points to all those matters which have been explained from the beginning to this point. All these matters are components of wisdom (hikmah). That is, these are those solid, established, and unchanging realities which possess strong foundations in intellect, human nature, and the shariah. Your Lord has revealed them to you through His Prophet so that you may reform your lives through them.

Reminder of the Theme of Monotheism: 'And do not make...'—this at the end again reminded of that theme of monotheism with which this discussion began in verse 22. It is as if monotheism stands as a fortress and wall for all these teachings. As long as this fortress is established, these teachings are also established; and if any breach occurred in this fortress, all these wise teachings will also vanish one by one. Examples of beginning with monotheism and ending with monotheism are abundant in the Qur'an, and indeed examples of this exist in the Torah as well, and we would have referred to them if there was no fear of drifting into other aspects of discussion.

The Ten Commandments of the Torah: Knowing how these matters are stated in the Torah will also be beneficial; for this reason, we quote the necessary part of it here: 'And the Lord spoke to Moses, saying, Speak to all the congregation of the children of Israel, and say to them, You shall be holy: for I the Lord your God am holy. You shall fear every man his mother, and his father, and keep my sabbaths: I am the Lord your God. Turn you not to idols, nor make to yourselves molten gods: I am the Lord your God. And if you offer a sacrifice of peace offerings to the Lord, you shall offer it at your own will... And when you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not wholly reap the corners of your field, neither shall you gather the gleanings of your harvest. And you shall not glean your vineyard, neither shall you gather every grape of your vineyard; you shall leave them for the poor and stranger: I am the Lord your God. You shall not steal, neither deal falsely, neither lie one to another. And you shall not swear by my name falsely, neither shall you profane the name of your God: I am the Lord. You shall not defraud your neighbor, neither rob him: the wages of him that is hired shall not abide with you all night until the morning. You shall not curse the deaf, nor put a stumbling block before the blind, but shall fear your God: I am the Lord. You shall do no unrighteousness in judgment: you shall not respect the person of the poor, nor honor the person of the mighty: but in righteousness shall you judge your neighbor. You shall not go up and down as a talebearer among your people: neither shall you stand against the blood of your neighbor: I am the Lord. You shall not hate your brother in your heart... You shall not avenge, nor bear any grudge against the children of your people, but you shall love your neighbor as yourself: I am the Lord. You shall keep my statutes.' (Leviticus, Chapter 19)

Subsequent Subject. Verses 40 to 57: The Actual Cause of the Disbelievers' Aversion to the Qur'an: In the subsequent verses, light is shed on the actual cause of the Quraysh's aversion to the Qur'an—showing that since they are not ready to abandon their assumed deities and accept the Hereafter, they harbor a grudge against the Qur'an and the Prophet. In this connection, as a parenthetical instruction, the Muslims are also directed to adopt a wise approach in propagating the message. They must not, under the influence of the opponents' behavior, utter any such word with the tongue that might cause further aversion to these startled people.

'Then has your Lord chosen you for [having] sons and taken [i.e., chosen] from among the angels daughters? Indeed, you say a grave saying.'

Refutation of Polytheism in a New Style: The previous set of verses concluded with the refutation of polytheism, and the same theme is taken up here in a new style. He stated: has your Lord chosen you for having sons and chosen daughters for Himself from among the angels? It must be understood that the Arab polytheists called the angels daughters of God and worshipped them under the illusion that they interceded for them with their Father. Allah has drawn attention here to their double foolishness—firstly, associating any partner with God is foolishness in itself; then, to make it worse, you have chosen daughters for God, whom you highly dislike for yourselves. 'Indeed, you say a grave saying'—meaning, this is indeed a highly absurd and grave statement you utter. This is not merely foolishness but double foolishness: you select for your Lord what you are not ready to accept for yourselves, as if you have degraded God even below yourselves.

'And We have certainly diversified in this Quran that they might be reminded, but it does not increase them except in aversion.'

'Tasreef' means to turn over or diversify. Here, it refers to presenting a reality in various styles and diverse ways. For example, the theme of monotheism has been explained in the Qur'an in so many different styles and ways that even the most simple-minded person can grasp it, provided he is not stubborn. But those who are stubborn and argumentative, not wanting to accept the truth, find their aversion and hatred increasing as the matter becomes clearer, because they consider its clarity as their defeat and humiliation. He stated: We have explained the reality of monotheism and the abomination of polytheism in this Qur'an in diverse ways so that these people may take heed. But the more they were treated, the more their disease increased.

'Say, 'If there had been with Him [other] gods, as they say, then they would have sought to the Owner of the Throne a way.'

The Arab polytheists, drawing a parallel with worldly kings and kingdoms, accepted Allah as the Owner of the throne and crown, i.e., the Supreme Deity, and accepted many other deities beneath Him, assuming they were partners in divinity and acted as a medium for their worshippers to gain proximity to the Owner of the Throne, securing whatever they wanted from Him. This is a refutation of their very illusion. He stated: if there had been other partners and associates with God, as you assume, they would have surely sought a way to dispute and contend with the Owner of the Throne, and this entire order of heaven and earth would have been disrupted. The meaning is: in the worldly kingdoms upon which you have based this concept of yours, you see that the maps of governments keep changing. If there were similarly partners and competitors with God, why would they sit silently? Why would they not exert their power to become the Owner of the Throne? Yet, here you see that neither has the sun shifted from its axis for a single day, nor has the earth deviated from its orbit. This reality is explained in another place thus: 'Had there been within them [i.e., the heavens and earth] gods besides Allah, they both would have been ruined.'

'Exalted is He and high above what they say by great sublimity.'

That is, Allah is completely free from and highly above such illusions and absurdities. These comparisons and metaphors have no relation to His sublime attributes.

'The seven heavens and the earth and whatever is in them exalt Him. And there is not a thing but that it exalts [Allah] by His praise, but you do not understand their exalting. Indeed, He is ever Forbearing and Forgiving.'

The Meaning of 'Exaltation' (Tasbeeh): The actual spirit of 'tasbeeh' is to declare God free from flaws (tanzeeh)—that is, to declare Him free from and above all those relations and attributes that are contrary to His sublime attributes and majesty. When it is joined with 'by His praise' (bi-hamdihi), as in 'it exalts [Allah] by His praise', it also carries the meaning of affirmation alongside tanzeeh—that is, declaring Him possessed of all sublime attributes.

The meaning is: within a specific sphere, God has granted you choice, and taking wrong advantage of this choice, you may attribute whatever falsehood you want to Him; but the seven heavens, the earth, and whatever creatures are within them all exalt God, though you do not understand their exaltation. 'Indeed, He is ever Forbearing and Forgiving'—meaning, your conduct is such that the heaven would have torn open upon you, but Allah is highly Forbearing and Forgiving, granting you respite despite these actions of yours.

'And when you recite the Quran, We place between you and those who do not believe in the Hereafter a concealed partition.'

The Actual Cause of the Disbelievers' Aversion to the Qur'an: This removes the wonder as to why a clear thing like the Qur'an—in which every matter has been explained in diverse ways, as stated in verse 41—is not understood by these people, and why they are so averted to it. He stated: these people neither believe in the Hereafter nor want to believe in it. This has become a hidden veil over their hearts, the effect of which is that the lights of the Qur'an cannot reflect upon their hearts.

'And We have placed over their hearts coverings, lest they understand it, and in their ears deafness. And when you mention your Lord in the Quran alone, they turn their backs in aversion.'

'Akinnah' is the plural of 'kinan', which means a covering. 'Lest they understand it' (an yafqahoohu) is originally 'karahata an yafqahoohu'—the prefix being omitted before 'an'. In 'and in their ears deafness' (wa fee aazaanihim waqra), 'lest they hear it' (an yasma'oohu) is omitted, as the context indicates.

This is a further explanation of the above subject—showing that We have placed coverings over their hearts so they do not understand the Qur'an, and deafness in their ears so they do not hear it. 'And when you mention your Lord in the Quran alone, they turn their backs in aversion'—this is the second major cause of their aversion to the Qur'an: since you mention only the One God in the Qur'an and give no status to their assumed deities, and these people are as averse to monotheism as they are to the Hereafter, they run away in aversion as soon as they hear the Qur'an.

The Divine Sunnah Regarding the Sealing of Hearts: The placing of coverings over hearts and deafness in ears points to that divine sunnah which has been described in Surah Al-Baqarah under the title of the sealing of hearts. We have explained every aspect of this divine sunnah in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. Those who seek details should read it; repeating it here would cause unnecessary length.

'We are most knowing of how they listen to it when they listen to you, and when they are in private conversation, when the wrongdoers say, 'You follow not but a man affected by magic.'

That is, they never listen to the Qur'an with the intention of understanding it and benefiting from it. They listen only to find some ground for objection and criticism, which they can then use to spread skepticism against it. He stated: We know their intention well, and We also know well their secret conversations when they say to the Muslims to turn them away from the Qur'an: 'You are following none but a man who is completely mad and affected by magic.'

'Look how they strike for you comparisons; but they have strayed, so they cannot [find] a way.'

'Striking comparisons' (darb-e-amthaal) here means casting remarks and sarcasms; see verses 8-9 of Surah Al-Furqan.

The meaning is: they are finding no way to raise any valid objection against you and the Qur'an, and because of this, they are completely lost—unable to stand or run. They do not want to believe, and they find no ground for refutation; so whatever comes to anyone's mouth, he utters it to vent his heart's grudge. One calls you a soothsayer, another a madman, some call you a magician, others affected by magic. The meaning is: show patience against the words of such bewildered people and leave them to their state. The actual reality will appear before them very soon.

'And they say, 'When we are bones and crumbled particles, will we truly be resurrected as a new creation?'

The Disbelievers' Disbelief in the Hereafter: This quotes their disbelief regarding the Hereafter—showing that they ask sarcastically and mockingly: 'When we are bones and crumbled particles, will we indeed be resurrected as a new creation?!'

Commentary on Verses 50 and 51: 'Say, 'Be you stones or iron. Or [any] creation of that which is great within your breasts.' And they will say, 'Who will restore us?' Say, 'He who created you the first time.' Then they will nod their heads toward you and say, 'When is that?' Say, 'Perhaps it will be soon.'

'Say, 'Be you stones or iron. Or [any] creation of that which is great within your breasts.' He directed: tell them that becoming bones and crumbled particles is nothing; even if you become stones or iron, or some even harder thing which in your view is completely devoid of the capability to accept life, you will still be resurrected. 'And they will say, 'Who will restore us?'' He stated: upon this statement of yours, they will ask: 'Who will restore us?' This is a question of denial—meaning, they will ask: 'Who can restore us after we have crumbled into dust?!' He taught the reply: 'Say, 'He who created you the first time.'—Tell them: 'The One Who created you the first time.' This reply is brief but complete. The meaning is: the God Who faced no difficulty in creating you from absolute nothingness, why would He be unable to bring you into existence again?

'Then they will nod their heads toward you and say, 'When is that?'—nodding of the head (inghad) means shaking the head. The meaning is: even after this silencing reply of yours, they are not people to remain silent, but after this, they will ask mockingly: 'When will this happen?' He taught the reply: 'Say, 'Perhaps it will be soon.'—Tell them: 'It is highly possible that its time has drawn near.' Implicit in this reply is the reality that as far as the exact time of the Resurrection is concerned, its knowledge belongs to none but Allah alone—to the extent that even the Prophet does not have its knowledge; but what is bound to happen is bound to happen. It will surely happen sooner or later, and it cannot be ignored merely because its exact time is not in our knowledge. Even the time of one's own death is not known to anyone, but only a fool would deny it on the basis that he does not know its time.

'On the Day He will call you and you will respond with praise of Him and think that you had not remained [in the world] except for a little.'

Now, addressing them directly, He stated: today you act very arrogantly, but remember that Day when the call will be made and you will run responding to this call while praising God. On that Day, all veils will be torn, and every reality will be seen with the eyes. Moreover, this period which lies between you and the Resurrection, and which appears very long to you today, will feel on that Day as if you had just slept and woken up. The meaning is: whoever dies, his Resurrection has virtually arrived, because there will be no feeling of the duration spent in the barzakh.

'And tell My servants to say that which is best. Indeed, Satan induces [dissension] among them. Indeed Satan is ever, to mankind, a clear enemy.'

Teaching the Wisdom of Propagation to the Muslims: It is completely clear from the verses above that in this era, the opponents had taken up all their weapons of sarcasm and mockery. Whenever an occasion for conversation arose, they would humiliate the Muslims and behave insolently and rudely with the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). An example of this has passed in verse 47 above. This situation required that instructions be given to the Muslims in this matter so they would not adopt any harsh approach under the influence of the disbelievers' behavior, which would be contrary to the temperament of the call. He directed: tell My servants (the Muslims) to say only that which is best—meaning, do not try to respond to the absurdities of the opponents; only convey the correct and true message, and remember that Satan—who is the open enemy of man—is constantly in wait for an opportunity to cast whispers and ruin the entire work. This very instruction was given for similar situations in Surah An-Nahl: 'Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good instruction, and argue with them in a way that is best. Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who has strayed from His way, and He is most knowing of who is [rightly] guided.' (An-Nahl: 125). This subject has also been explained in verses 198 to 201 of Surah Al-A'raf; take a look at them if further detail is required.

'Your Lord is most knowing of you. If He wills, He will have mercy upon you; or if He wills, He will punish you. And We have not sent you, [O Muhammad], over them as a guardian.'

The Limit of the Believers' Responsibility in Propagation: This clarifies the limit of the believers' and the Prophet's responsibility in the matter of propagation so that their hearts do not feel constrained by the hostile behavior of the disbelievers. He stated: God alone knows best who is deserving of mercy—and he will receive guidance and deserve mercy; and who is deserving of punishment—and he will remain established on misguidance and deserve punishment. The responsibility of the Prophet and his companions is solely to convey the call; they do not hold the responsibility to force everyone to believe. The meaning is: you have not been appointed as a warden over them, such that you have to account for their disbelief or faith. Your responsibility is solely to convey the truth; you perform that, and accepting or not accepting it is their job, and they will be held accountable for it before God, not you.
