So, why do you worry unnecessarily?

'And your Lord is most knowing of whoever is in the heavens and the earth. And We have preferred some of the prophets over others, and We gave David the Psalms [i.e., the Zaboor].'

The Correct Perspective Regarding the Preference of Prophets: In the heat of debate, the thing that mostly causes mischief is the prejudiced preference and comparison of one's respective leaders. Whomever one follows, one associates all excellence only with him, and in accepting any excellence for another, one feels humiliation and defeat. It appears that in this era, this mischief had also arisen, or there was a fear of its arising—particularly because the Jews, as mentioned before, had entered the field of opposition with all their plots and were supporting the opponents. The Jews, as explained under verse 253 of Surah Al-Baqarah, had a specific interest in this mischief. To crush this mischief, the Qur'an taught the Muslims: Allah alone knows best whoever is in the heavens and the earth; He alone knows what is everyone's rank and who is elevated to what status. Other people do not know this. As to the question of who among the prophets has been preferred over whom, Allah preferred some of His prophets over others in various ways. For example, Allah spoke to Moses, He gave Jesus, the son of Mary, clear proofs and supported him with the Pure Spirit, and He gave David the Psalms. This is the correct perspective regarding the prophets, and the Muslims are directed to remain firm on it: they should express and declare the specific distinctions of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and also accept the specific distinctions of the other prophets. Take a look also at what we have written under verse 253 of Surah Al-Baqarah regarding this issue. The specific distinction of the Psalms (Zaboor) given to David is that among the heavenly scriptures, this is the only book in poetic form, comprising entirely songs of praise and glorification.

'Say, 'Invoke those you have claimed besides Him, for they do not possess the ability for removal of adversity from you or for [its] transfer.' 

Connection of the Speech with the Subject Above: The previous three verses came—as we have stated—incidentally as a parenthetical warning and instruction; now the speech connects back to its original sequence, i.e., verses 51-52. Above it was stated: 'Perhaps it will be soon.' Now He directed: if you assume that those whom you make partners besides God will help you, call them; neither can they remove any adversity from you, nor can they temporarily transfer it to anyone else.

'Those whom they invoke seek means of access to their Lord, [striving as to] which of them would be nearest, and they hope for His mercy and fear His punishment. Indeed, the punishment of your Lord is ever feared.' That is, those angels whom they make partners with God are themselves busy at all times in seeking proximity to God; they hope for His mercy and fear His punishment—and why should they not fear? The punishment of your Lord is indeed something that everyone must fear and guard against, no matter how high his status.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 58 to 60: The Divine Sunnah Regarding Punishment: It has passed in verse 11 above that when the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) warned the polytheists of divine punishment, they immediately demanded: 'Why do you not bring that punishment with which you are constantly threatening us?' Now, the divine sunnah in this matter is explained—showing why Allah does not send down signs of punishment upon people's demand. Read the verses:

'And there is no city but We will destroy it before the Day of Resurrection or punish it with a severe punishment. That has ever been in the Book written.'

'Qaryah' refers to central towns which are the centers of the elite and wealthy of a nation.

Answer to the Demand for the Sign of Punishment: This is the answer to the Quraysh's demand for the sign of punishment—showing that they should not make haste; every town that chooses the path of disbelief and rebellion will surely face one of two situations: either We will destroy it completely, or We will punish it severely. This point is stated here in brief, but the divine sunnah under which this happens has been explained in verse 16 above: 'And when We intend to destroy a city, We command its affluent to obey, but they defiantly disobey therein; so the word [i.e., decree] deserves to be passed against it, and We destroy it with [complete] destruction.' Although this divine sunnah has always been active, the matter became even more severe for the Quraysh because Allah had raised a messenger from among themselves. A messenger is the ultimate medium for the completion of proof. If a nation denies its messenger, it is surely destroyed after a specific period of respite. 'That has ever been in the Book written'—meaning, this is recorded in the divine decree. He has written that such and such nation will commit such and such crimes of its own choice, and as a punishment, it will meet its end.

'And nothing has prevented Us from sending signs except that the former peoples denied them. And We gave Thamud the she-camel as a visible sign, but they wronged her. And We send not the signs except as a warning.'

The Wisdom Behind Not Sending Signs: This explains the wisdom and mercy due to which Allah is not sending any sign of punishment despite their intense demand. He stated that the purpose of signs is to warn and admonish people so they may fear that divine punishment of which they are being informed; but previous nations, through their bad deeds, always denied these signs and thereby opened the door of divine punishment upon themselves. 'And We gave Thamud the she-camel as a visible sign, but they wronged her'—since the preposition 'bi' (with/her) does not fit with the verb 'zalamoo' (they wronged), an omission is assumed: 'zalamoo anfusahum wa kazzaboo biha'—they wronged their own selves and denied the she-camel's being a sign.

An Example of the Denial of Signs: This is an example of the aforementioned point—showing how previous nations denied the signs of punishment. He stated: We gave the people of Thamud the she-camel as a clear, eye-opening sign. If they had not been blinded by stubbornness, it was sufficient for them; but far from benefiting from this sign, they cut her houghs and killed her, thereby opening the door of divine punishment upon themselves. 'And We send not the signs except as a warning'—meaning, whenever We send a sign of punishment, We send it merely to warn and admonish against the actual punishment; but stubborn people are not convinced by it, and they always make some excuse to continue in their self-indulgence. If a dust-storm pelting stones (hasib) rises from some side, they will say: 'This is a cloud of mercy that is going to rain upon us.' If some other earthly or heavenly calamity appears, they will say: 'Such soft and hard days have always come upon nations.' In short, any sign intended merely for warning and admonishing cannot convince them; they can be convinced only by the actual punishment itself, but after its appearance, the question of being convinced or not remains of no use.

'And [remember, O Muhammad], when We told you, 'Indeed, your Lord has encompassed the people.' And We did not make the sight which We showed you except as a trial for the people, and [likewise] the accursed tree [i.e., the zaqqum] in the Quran. And We threaten [i.e., warn] them, but it increases them not except in great transgression.'

The Quraysh's Attitude Regarding Warning Signs: The previous verses pointed to the attitude of previous nations regarding warning signs. Now, this refers to some matters of the Prophet's own nation, showing that if they were shown any warning sign upon their demand, their attitude would be no different from that of previous nations. They too would deny it, because they have mocked all the warnings given to them until now. For example, when We informed you that your Lord has encompassed the people, they mocked it instead of taking warning, claiming: 'You are threatening us and boasting.'

This points to those verses in which Allah informed that We are reducing the territory of the disbelievers from its borders towards Makkah. For example, in Surah Ar-Ra'd: 'Have they not seen that We set upon the land, reducing it from its borders?' This was stated when Islam was spreading around Makkah, and Makkah and its people were slowly being encompassed by Islam. The same is in verse 44 of Surah Al-Anbiya. Then, when the era of victories began, the Qur'an foretold the victory of Makkah in these words: 'And [We promise] other [victories] which you were not [yet] able to take, but Allah has already encompassed them.' These were not hollow words, but the circumstances were clearly foretelling them; yet the stubborn leaders of the Quraysh denied all these warnings until the very end, when they had to surrender before the Muslims. 'And We did not make the sight which We showed you except as a trial for the people'—this is the second example of their stubbornness. He stated that the vision We showed you also contained warning and admonition, but it also became merely a trial for them, and they mocked it.

Reaction to the Event of Mi'raj: 'Sight' (Ru'ya) here points to the event of Mi'raj which has been mentioned at the beginning of the Surah. There we explained that within this event lay a warning for both the Quraysh and the Children of Israel—showing that the guardianship and trust of both the Sacred House and the Furthest House was about to be snatched from their current corrupt guardians and handed over to the Gentile Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his followers. But neither group benefited from this warning. Each mocked it in various ways, to the extent that the thing intended for their warning became merely a trial for them due to their bad deeds.

(We have explained before the difference between the vision and the dream of prophets. The vision of prophets is a true vision, which is among the sources of divine revelation. The Prophet was shown several major events through vision. The observations of vision are sometimes more reliable than the observations of physical eyes. The Prophet saw all the observations shown to him on the night of Mi'raj, and his eyes never dazzled.)

Another Example of Not Benefiting from Warnings: 'and [likewise] the accursed tree [i.e., the zaqqum] in the Quran'—this is the third example of their not benefiting from warnings. 'Accursed tree' (Shajarah Mal'oonah) points to the tree of Zaqqum, regarding which it has come in the Qur'an that it will be in Hell, and the people of Hell will eat from it out of hunger, and then drink boiling water upon it like thirsty camels. The attribute 'accursed' (mal'oonah) used for the tree is the opposite of 'blessed' (mubarakah). A blessed tree is one that benefits people through its shade, freshness, and fruit. Completely opposite to this is an accursed tree, which has no shade or fruit, but is merely a heap of thorns, bitterness, and poison—a curse and misery for people. The description of Zaqqum is the same.

Obviously, these descriptions of Hell were narrated so that heedless people might take warning and do what they can today to save themselves from it; but the self-styled wise of the Quraysh, instead of benefiting from this warning, began to raise this point by comparing the matters of the next world with the matters of this world: 'Look, this person is gathering fire, water, and trees in one place! How is it possible that there is fire and also a tree in it, fire and also water in it?' 'And We threaten them, but it increases them not except in great transgression'—meaning, We are informing them of future dangers through these matters so they may think of saving themselves from them; but these things, instead of benefiting them, are increasing them in that very great transgression in which they are involved. The meaning is: do not expect those whose minds have become so crooked that even a straight thing becomes crooked within them to believe if they are shown a sign upon their demand. They will find some excuse even after seeing it. These are not people who can benefit from a sign of punishment, but are people who can be convinced only by the actual punishment itself, after the appearance of which the question of belief remains of no use.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 61 to 65: The Actual Cause of Arrogance: In the following, the actual cause of these people's transgression and arrogance is uncovered—showing that its actual cause is not what they claim: that they have not been shown a sign of punishment; rather, it is that when Allah blessed them with His favors, they became proud and arrogant instead of being grateful servants. In choosing this path, they followed strictly in the footsteps of Iblees, and proved true his assumption regarding the offspring of Adam. Read the verses:

'And [remember] when We said to the angels, 'Prostrate to Adam,' and they prostrated except Iblees. He said, 'Should I prostrate to one You created from clay?'

All components of this story of Adam and Iblees have been discussed under the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah and Al-A'raf; for this reason, we restrict our discussion here to the context of the speech.

The Actual Cause of the Disbelievers' Denial: This is consolation for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—showing that the actual cause of these people's denial is not that they are not being shown a sign of punishment, but the actual cause is their arrogance, and in this matter, they have followed strictly the path of their leader, Iblees. Just as when he was commanded to prostrate to Adam, he refused by stating: 'How can I, a superior creature, prostrate to a creature of dust?' similarly, these people have also become blind in the pride of leadership and wealth upon receiving God's favors, and this pride of theirs is not allowing them to accept you as a messenger, accepting your superiority over them, and abandoning their pride of leadership.

'[Iblees] said, 'Spoken [to Me], this one whom You have honored above me? If You delay me [i.e., my death] until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his descendants, except for a few.''

The style of address in 'Spoken' (Ara'aytaka) is used for sarcasm and contempt, and 'ihtanakal-jaradu al-arda' means that the locusts cleared all the vegetation of the land.

The Lesson of the Story of Adam and Iblees: That is, this hostility between Adam and Iblees has not ended, but is going to last until the Day of Resurrection. In his jealousy, he demanded respite from God until the Day of Resurrection, claiming: 'If You grant me respite, I will surely clear and destroy all the offspring of Adam, proving that Adam and his offspring do not deserve any honor above me, and that I was completely in the right in refusing to prostrate to them.'

The purpose of referring to this is to show that the reason why these people are not believing is that they have fallen into the clutches of Iblees and his offspring, and he has proved true the claim he made on the first day regarding these people.

'[Allah] said, 'Go, for whoever of them follows you, then indeed Hell will be your recompense, an ample recompense.'

That is, the respite Iblees demanded from God to mislead the offspring of Adam until the Day of Resurrection, God granted it to him: 'Go, do whatever you can do; Hell is sufficient to pay the full recompense of you and those who follow you.' That is, granting you this respite does not carry any fear of the opportunity being lost such that any deficiency remains in your punishment, because Hell is something that will pay all the balance at once.

'And incite [to senselessness] whoever you can among them with your voice and assault them with your horses and foot soldiers and share with them in wealth and children and promise them.' But Satan does not promise them except delusion.'

'Istifzaz' means to panic, unsettle, and uproot; and 'voice' (sawt) here refers to noise, clamor, and propaganda.

The Limit of Iblees's Respite: This points to the limit of the respite given to Iblees and his offspring to carry out their campaign of misguidance, so that people do not treat it as a simple matter, but those who want to save their faith from his trials remain always alert to face him.

The Diversity of Satan's Temptations: 'And incite [to senselessness] whoever you can among them with your voice.' That is, go, use whatever noise, clamor, slogans, propaganda, music, movies, singing, assemblies, processions, speeches, announcements, newspapers, magazines, and all such things you can to turn people away from the straight path, and uproot whomever you can. 'and assault them with your horses and foot soldiers'—'khayl' refers to horsemen and 'rajl' refers to foot soldiers. That is, bring also the horsemen and foot soldiers of your army of misguidance to turn them away from faith. Note that bringing horsemen and foot soldiers is not merely a metaphor, but is also a physical reality. All those wars which the enemies of Islam waged to turn the believers away from the true religion are included in this. 'and share with them in wealth and children'—that is, go and become a partner in the wealth and children We have provided to people, if you can. Your worshippers will make you a partner in them: they will set apart your share from their wealth, name their children after you, and sacrifice some of them for your pleasure. 'and promise them. But Satan does not promise them except delusion.'—that is, show them sweet and golden promises of green pastures so they may become completely free from the actual responsibilities of life. After this, He gave this warning: 'But Satan does not promise them except delusion'—meaning, all the promises Satan makes are merely deception. For example, the polytheists' illusion that the angels they worship will bring them closer to God, or the Jews' illusion that being the sons and beloved of God, the fire of Hell will not touch them.

'Indeed, over My servants, [O Iblees], you have no authority.' And sufficient is your Lord as a Disposer of affairs.'

Consolation to Those Who Remain Firm on Faith: 'Sultan' means authority, strength, and power. Allah also made it clear to Iblees: you have indeed been given respite to create all these trials, but you will not possess the authority to turn away by force those of My servants who want to remain firm on My worship. This is consolation for those who remain firm on faith—showing that whatever Satan does, he does not possess this absolute authority to turn whoever he wants away from faith. Absolute authority belongs solely to Allah. Then, for further consolation, He stated: 'And sufficient is your Lord as a Disposer of affairs'—meaning, those servants of Allah who want to remain firm on their faith despite all Satan's trials, and hand themselves over to their Lord with complete trust—God is their disposer of affairs, and He is sufficient as a disposer of affairs. He will protect His servant under the most difficult conditions and save his faith.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 66 to 72: An Illustration of the Arrogant and Heedless: In the following, there is an illustration of the state of man when he is caught in some calamity: he calls out to God, weeping and supplicating before Him; but as soon as he is saved from that calamity, he begins to act arrogantly and rebelliously again. He completely forgets that if God wills, He can catch him again in the same state, and catch him in such a way that he can never find escape. After this, there is an illustration of two groups: those who look at the world with open eyes and obtain correct lessons from it, and those who remain always blind. The end of both these types of people in the Hereafter is described. All this description and illustration is of the polytheists of the Quraysh who were the primary addressees of the Qur'an, but since this is the behavior of all rebellious ones, the statement is put in general terms. Read the verses:

Commentary on Verses 66 and 67: 'Your Lord is He who drives for you the ships through the sea that you may seek of His bounty. Indeed, He is ever, to you, Merciful. And when adversity touches you at sea, lost are they you invoke except for Him. But when He delivers you to the land, you turn away [from Him]. And ever is man ungrateful.'

Undervaluing Allah's Blessings: This is an illustration of the fact that every blessing man receives comes from Allah alone. Its requirement is that man should benefit from this blessing and remain grateful to his Lord; but it is the strange misfortune of man that when he receives the blessing, he acts arrogantly towards God, considering everything as the result of his own efforts and strategies and the favor of his assumed deities. But when he is caught in some adversity, he begins to call out to God, and at that time, all his deities are forgotten. Then, when Allah saves him from that adversity, his previous arrogance returns, and he places God on the shelf of forgetfulness again.

The Example of the Ship and the Sea: To explain this reality, the example of the ship and the sea is given: it is God's power alone that a ship of thousands of tons sails on the chest of the sea. Allah made this arrangement so that man may benefit from it in his journeys and remain grateful to Him for His favor and mercy; but the state of man is such that as long as the ship sails smoothly, he never thinks of God; but when the ship is caught in a storm and begins to toss, he forgets his arrogance and his other deities, and at that time, he calls out only to God. But this state remains only as long as the ship is in the whirlpool of calamity. As soon as the ship emerges from the whirlpool and man steps onto the dry land, he forgets both that hour of calamity and his weeping and supplicating before God.

In this illustration, there is also consolation for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—showing that do not assume that if these rebellious ones of the Quraysh are shown the sign of punishment they demand, they will adopt the path of faith and guidance; rather, when they are caught in adversity, they will call out to God, but as soon as they are saved, they will return to their previous arrogance. The path of faith and guidance is that man listen to the voice of his nature and accept the guidance of his intellect. Since they are not ready for this, how can any sign of punishment lead them to the path of guidance?

Some Questions to the Rebellious: 'Then do you feel secure that He will not cause a part of the land to swallow you or send against you a storm of stones? Then you would not find for yourselves any protector.'

Now, this question is asked: after emerging from the sea onto the dry land, why do you become secure and unconcerned regarding God? Do you assume that God's sovereignty is limited only to the sea, and the dry land is outside His sovereignty? If He causes the land to swallow you up, or sends against you a fierce wind pelting stones which destroys you and your houses, who can protect you from God?

'Or do you feel secure that He will not send you back into it [i.e., the sea] another time and send upon you a hurricane of wind and drown you for what you disbelieved? Then you would not find for yourselves against Us any avenger.'

'Qasif' means a breaking wind, and 'tabee'' means a supporter and avenger. That is, having escaped from the sea once, why do you assume that you have become free from God forever? After all, it is also possible that to punish you for your ingratitude, God creates such circumstances that you have to face the same sea again, and He sends a hurricane of wind upon you which breaks everything and drowns you, and no supporter of yours stands to pursue Us in your support! In verse 75 ahead, the same theme is expressed in these words: 'Then you would not find for yourself against Us any helper.'

'And We have certainly honored the children of Adam and carried them on the land and sea and provided for them of the good things and preferred them over much of what We have created, with [definite] preference.'

Reminding Man of His Responsibility: Now, man is reminded of his responsibility—showing that We have honored man, arranged سواری (riding) for him on both land and sea, provided him with pure sustenance, and preferred him over much of Our creation; so his duty is to recognize the right of these blessings, and instead of acting arrogantly and showing ingratitude upon receiving these blessings, he should remain a grateful and obedient servant of his Lord. Acting arrogantly upon receiving blessings is the path of Iblees, and his fate is well known. 'and preferred them over much of what We have created, with [definite] preference' also makes it clear that the preference man enjoys over other creatures is not absolute. Among God's creatures, there are creatures over whom man does not enjoy preference.

'[Mention, O Muhammad], the Day We will call all people with their leader [i.e., record of deeds]. Then whoever is given his record in his right hand - those will read their records, and injustice will not be done to them, [even] as much as a thread.'

'Anaas' means groups of humans, and 'imam' refers to leaders and guides.

Reminder of the Day of Recompense: This is a reminder of that Day when the divine court of recompense will be established. He stated: on that Day, We will command every group to present themselves before Us along with their leaders and guides. The righteous will present themselves with their righteous leaders and guides, and the wicked will be brought with their corrupt leaders. Then, the righteous will be given their records of deeds in their right hands, and the wicked in their left hands. Those who receive their records in their right hands will read them, and they will see that not even a thread of injustice has been done to them. Their every single good deed, whether small or large, is recorded, and they have received its full reward. 'They will read their records'—here the verb is used in its literal meaning. That is, they will read everything with joy, offering gratitude to their Lord for His grace and favor. Although there is no explicit mention here of the feelings of those who receive their records in their left hands, the context clearly indicates that instead of reading their records, they will beat their heads and faces: 'Woe to us that not a single point in our record is bright; there is only darkness.'

Gathering every group with their leaders and guides contains both an aspect of honor and respect for the righteous, and an aspect of the completion of proof. The leaders of the righteous will see that, thank God, the cause of elevating the word of Allah for which they sacrificed has succeeded in this grand form; and the leaders of the wicked will see the results of their plots, and their followers will curse them, demanding double punishment for them, as explicitly stated in other places.

The End of the People of the Left: 'And whoever is in this [life] blind will be in the Hereafter blind and more astray in path.' After the people of the right, this describes the end of the people of the left—showing that since these people remained blind in the world, they will rise blind in the Hereafter too, and the distance they have from the actual destination today will become even greater in the Hereafter, because after the appearance of the Hereafter, there will remain no possibility for them to return to the straight path.

This verse also sheds light on the reality that the rank and position the people of the right will obtain will be because they did not live in the world with their eyes closed, but always kept their eyes open and benefited fully from every single sign of Allah.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 73 to 87: Exhortation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to Remain Firm on the Stand of Truth: In the following, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is directed with emphasis: remain firm on your stand of truth despite all the opposition of the opponents. They will exert all their power to make you agree to some compromise regarding the Qur'an, and then they will become your close friends. But you have no right to make even a hair's breadth of change in it. The most they can do in opposing you is to harass you so much that you are forced to leave this city. If this happens, then after you, they too will not be able to remain in this city for long. The fate that befell previous nations after the migration of their messengers will be theirs too. For obtaining patience and steadfastness, emphasis is placed on prayer, particularly Tahajjud, and the prayer of migration is taught, pointing to the victory associated with migration. At the end, regret is expressed over the misfortune of the opponents—showing that a thing like the Qur'an, which is entirely cure and mercy, became a source of loss for them due to their bad deeds. Read the verses:

Commentary on Verses 73 to 75: 'And indeed, they were about to tempt you away from that which We revealed to you in order to [make you] invent about Us something else; and then they would have taken you as a friend. And if We had not strengthened you, you would have almost inclined to them a little. Then [if you had], We would have made you taste double [punishment] in life and double after death. Then you would not find for yourself against Us any helper.'

'Yaftinoonaka' (they tempt you) here carries the meaning of turning away (yasrifoonaka), as indicated by the preposition 'an' (from). The meaning is: the opponents had exerted so much power that they were about to put you into trials to turn you away from the stand of truth, but Allah saved you from this trial.

Answer to the Disbelievers' Offer of Compromise: In the call of the messengers, a stage has occurred when the opponents, realizing that the call has taken root and cannot be shaken merely by blind opposition, have played this political card: they have proposed a mutual compromise. This stage occurred in the call of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) too. The opponents, realizing that the call was taking root in hearts, proposed through some of their groups—the tribe of Banu Thaqeef is mentioned in reports—that if you make such and such changes in the commands, we will accept this call, and then we and you will remain close friends. This stage was highly severe for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). On one hand were the commands revealed by Allah, in which you had no authority to make even a single change; on the other hand, you were highly desirous of your nation's faith and did not want to lose any opportunity that showed any hope of their coming to the path of faith. This situation put the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) into temporary anxiety. The words 'you would have almost inclined to them a little' point to this very anxiety. Allah supported His Prophet in this delicate stage, took him out of anxiety, and established him on the correct highway.

The Meaning of 'Prophetic Infallibility' (Ismah): Keep in view that the infallibility of a prophet does not mean that he never faces any stage of anxiety or that no wrong inclination ever crosses his mind; rather, it means that firstly, his inclination is never towards personal desires but is always towards the side of goodness; secondly, even on the side of goodness, if he appears to take any step which is not correct, Allah protects him from it and guides him to the correct direction.

Address to the Prophet, Warning to the Disbelievers: 'Then [if you had], We would have made you taste...'—in terms of outward words, the address is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), but the severe warning in it is directed towards those who proposed the compromise, so they may be warned that when even the Prophet is threatened with double punishment in this world and the Hereafter for any such step, what possibility remains of its being implemented. In 'double [punishment] in life and double after death' (di'fal-hayati wa di'fal-mamat), a prefix is omitted: 'di'fa 'azaab-il-hayati wa di'fa 'azaab-il-mamat'—and this threat of double punishment is according to the status of the messenger, whose accountability is also severe if he commits any slip.

Commentary on Verses 76 and 77: 'And indeed, they were about to provoke [i.e., drive] you from the land [i.e., Makkah] to-drive you out from it. And then they would not have remained [there] after you, except for a little. [This is] the established way [i.e., sunnah] of those We had sent before you of Our messengers; and you will not find in Our way any alteration.'

'Istifzaz' means to panic, disturb, and uproot; and 'the land' (Al-Ard) here refers to Makkah.

When the opponents were disappointed regarding any possibility of compromise, their opposition—which was already intense—became twofold. They exerted all their power to uproot your steps from Makkah so you would be forced to leave. Upon this, He stated: if they force you to leave, they too will not be able to remain here for long after you. The fate that befell previous nations after the migration of their messengers will surely be theirs too. There is no reason why the sunnah of Allah which has been active from before regarding this matter should change for them.

Until the Migration of the Messenger, the Nation is Secure: We have explained in another place that since the messenger is the ultimate medium for the completion of proof, and as long as he remains among the nation, he keeps praying for their forgiveness, the nation enjoys security from divine punishment; but when he is forced to migrate due to the nation's conduct, the nation is left like a body without a soul, and the stage of the completion of proof concludes. After this, either some divine punishment appears which cleanses the land of this crop of corruption, or the sword of the believers is unsheathed to put an end to them. In the case of the wicked of Makkah, this second situation occurred.

In our view, 'established way' (Sunnata) is in the accusative case due to an omitted verb pointing to 'Remember the sunnah...': 'Remember the sunnah of those We sent before you of Our messengers and their nations.' This style contains an aspect of specific mention, bringing the actual matter directly before the eyes.

'Establish prayer at the decline of the sun [i.e., Al-Duluq] until the darkness of the night and [also] the Quran [i.e., recitation] of dawn. Indeed, the recitation of dawn is ever witnessed.'

'Establishing prayer' (iqamat-us-salat) does not merely mean performing prayer, but means maintaining the prayer. 'Duluq' means decline. The decline of the sun has three stages. First, when it declines from the meridian. Second, when it sinks below the horizon of sight. Third, when it disappears from the horizon. These three times are for the Zuhr, Asr, and Maghrib prayers. The preposition 'li' (at) in 'li-duluq' is in the sense of time. The use of 'li' for this meaning is well known in Arabic. The meaning which we express with 'at' is expressed in some places by 'li'—for example, 'as-salatu li-waqtiha' means prayer at its time; 'aqimi-salata li-duluq-is-shams' means maintain prayer at the decline of the sun. 'Ghasaq-il-layl' refers to the darkness of the early night when it becomes intense; this is the time for the Isha prayer. 'Wa qur'an-al-fajr'—although this can be placed under the command 'aqim', in my view, its accusative case is due to specific mention, meaning: 'specifically mention the Qur'an of dawn.' This specific mention highlights the importance of the Fajr prayer. 'Indeed, the recitation of dawn is ever witnessed'—recitation (qur'an) here refers to the recitation of the Qur'an in the Fajr prayer. This word points to the length of recitation which is specific to the Fajr prayer for both the imam and the followers, and to the presence of the angels which is among the blessings of this blessed time and which is mentioned in reports.

From this, we learn that the mention of the five prayers and their times is present in the Qur'an itself. Furthermore, from the subsequent verse, we learn that the mention of Tahajjud and its time is also in the Qur'an. After such clear verses, if anyone says that there is no mention of the five prayers in the Qur'an, what else can be said of such stubborn people except that Allah guide them.

The Bowing and Prostration of Cosmic Objects: In appointing the times of prayers, it appears that Allah has kept in view the times of the bowing and prostration of the major signs of this universe. This verse indicates that the sun remains in standing, bowing, and prostrating throughout the times of day and night. Clearly, the same is the case with all those things upon which the sun's reflection falls. They also bow and prostrate before their Lord along with it. This reality is pointed out in the verses: 'and their shadows in the mornings and the afternoons' and 'there is not a thing but that it exalts [Allah] by His praise'. All these creatures invite man through their state: just as they bow and prostrate before God, he too should join them in praising his Lord; but it is the strange misfortune of man that he bows his head before the most useless of creatures, yet acts arrogantly towards his actual Lord before Whom even his worshipped things are constantly bowing and prostrating. Take this sun, whose bowing and prostrating is mentioned here—who can estimate how much it has been and is being worshipped in the world?

Prayer is for Obtaining Patience and Steadfastness: The emphasis on maintaining prayer here, as we indicated above, is for obtaining patience and steadfastness in this difficult stage which was then being faced by the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Muslims. In the severe trials faced in the path of truth, steadfastness on the truth is impossible without the support of Allah, and the greatest means of obtaining His support is prayer, particularly the Tahajjud prayer, which is mentioned ahead.

'And from [part of] the night, pray with it [i.e., Al-Tehajjud] as additional [worship] for you; it is expected that your Lord will resurrect you to a praised station.'

The Meaning of 'Tahajjud': 'Tahajjud' literally means to wake up after sleeping for a part of the night, but in Qur'an's terminology, it refers to that prayer which is performed after waking up from sleep in the night. In 'bihi' (with it), 'bi' in my view is adverbial (zarfiyah), and the pronoun points to 'night' (layl).

The Meaning of 'Nafl' (Additional): 'Nafilah' refers to something additional to the original. It is used for an increase in blessing and mercy, not for an increase in burden and hardship. That is, this prayer is additional for you besides the five obligatory prayers. 'For you' (laka) indicates that as far as the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was concerned, this prayer was obligatory upon him. Consequently, he maintained it throughout his life. The emphasis on maintaining prayers, as we indicated, was for obtaining strength to face the satanic forces. For this purpose, Tahajjud was emphasized, and was termed 'nafilatan laka'—meaning, this is additional help for you which will increase your patience and steadfastness in facing the difficulties of the path. Although this prayer is not obligatory for ordinary believers, for those who rise to face the satanic forces and establish the true religion in the world, this prayer is the greatest means of obtaining God's help. Consequently, the righteous of the nation who were blessed with serving the religion have always maintained this prayer.

'Asaa' is originally used for expressing hope, expectation, and strong assumption, but when it is used with reference to Allah, the hope and expectation relates to the addressee or speaker, not to Allah. For example, 'asaa rabbukum an yarhamakum' is translated as: 'expect that your Lord will have mercy upon you.' 'asaa-allahu an ya'tiyani bihim jamee'a' is translated as: 'I hope that Allah will bring all of them to me.' Similarly, 'asaa an yab'athaka rabbuka maqamam-mahmooda' is translated as: 'expect that your Lord will resurrect you to a praised station.' This removes the doubt raised by those unfamiliar with Arabic grammar—how can hope and expectation be attributed to God when everything is known to Him? In its proper place, we will clarify that 'la'alla' (perhaps) and similar words also carry the same meaning when used with reference to God.

Praised Station (Maqam-e-Mahmood): 'Maqam' in our view is not adverbial (of place), but is an infinitive, standing as a cognate accusative (maf'ool mutlaq). Since there is a common meaning in the words 'ba'th' (resurrecting) and 'maqam' (rising/standing)—the former meaning to raise and the latter to stand—there is no objection to its being a cognate accusative. The meaning is: today, there is a loud noise of opposition and condemnation against you; but remain firm on your stand of truth, maintain prayers, particularly Tahajjud, and expect that your Lord will resurrect you in a state when the tongue of a great nation will sing your praise, and your efforts will be praised and appreciated before Allah.

'And say, 'My Lord, cause me to enter a sound entrance and to exit a sound exit and grant me from Yourself a supporting authority.'

The word 'qul' (say) is used here in the sense of making a prayer. This word has occurred in this sense in several places in the Qur'an; see verse 29 of Surah Al-Mu'minoon. The actual spirit of the word 'sidq' (sound/truth), as we have clarified in another place, is honor, stability, and firmness. 'Sultan' means dominance and establishing power.

An Indication to the Proximity of Migration and a Great Glad Tiding: It has become clear from the verses above that circumstances had become so severe that the Prophet's migration from Makkah was now inevitable; but the Messenger of Allah does not migrate without Allah's permission. For this reason, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) remained firm on his call and his position. This is the first time you are taught this prayer, which not only points to the proximity of migration but also contains the glad tiding that before your exit, arrangements have been made for your entry, and both your exit and entry will be with honor, stability, and firmness, and that the special divine help and dominance will accompany you in this journey. Placing 'cause me to enter' (adkhilnee) before 'to exit' (akhrijnee) in the prayer points to the fact that the arrangement for your entry has been made before your exit, and 'from Yourself' (min ladunka) indicates that no matter how unfavorable and hostile the outward conditions are, your Lord Himself will make all arrangements for you. Consider: while in terms of outward words, this is a prayer taught to you, in reality, it is a great glad tiding given to you under highly dangerous conditions. Read the history of migration and the conditions of the Prophet's entry into Madinah; you will find that the creation saw every word of this glad tiding manifesting physically before their eyes.

'And say, 'Truth has come, and falsehood has departed. Indeed is falsehood, [by its nature], ever bound to depart.'

'Truth' (Al-Haqq) refers to the Qur'an and the true religion with which the Qur'an came, and 'falsehood' (Al-Batil) refers to the false religion which the Qur'an came to eradicate.

Declaration of the Victory of Truth and Defeat of Falsehood: Above, you were taught the prayer of migration, and now under those very delicate conditions, you are commanded to declare the victory of truth and the defeat of falsehood. The reason is what we pointed out in its proper place: migration in reality is the preface of the Messenger's victory. After it, the opponents of the Messenger are surely eradicated, and the true religion is bound to rise. 'Indeed is falsehood, [by its nature], ever bound to depart' points to the reality that falsehood has no foundation within human nature. It spreads like wild bushes only when there are no people to cleanse it. When the people of truth appear to cleanse it—though they have to suffer struggle according to circumstances—it is bound to perish, and in its place, the crop of truth which the people of truth sow will flourish, because the soil of human nature has been created by its Creator to nourish this crop of truth, not to nourish the weeds of ignorance.

At the victory of Makkah, this prophecy was realized. At that time, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) broke the idols of the Kaaba with his staff, reciting this verse, showing that the true meaning of the verse had manifested physically.

'And We send down of the Quran that which is healing and mercy for the believers, but it does not increase the wrongdoers except in loss.'

The Deprivation of Those Who Deny the Qur'an: This expresses regret and blame over the deprivation and misfortune of those who deny the Qur'an—showing that whatever We are revealing of the Qur'an contains the cure for all their spiritual and intellectual diseases, and is entirely mercy in terms of its ultimate outcome, but for those who wrong their own selves, this very thing becomes a cause of further loss, because they establish the proof against themselves and make themselves deserving of severe punishment in both this world and the Hereafter.

'And when We bestow favor upon man, he turns away and distances himself; and when evil touches him, he is distracting.' The word 'man' (insan), though general, refers to those wicked of the Quraysh whose conduct is under discussion here. They adopted the path of arrogant aversion to Allah's Book and His Messenger, as clear from 'turns away and distances himself' (a'rada wa na'aa bi-janibihi), so Allah also turned away from them and spoke in a general form. In 'turns away and distances himself', the physical posture of their aversion is depicted: one can turn away gracefully, but when a person turns away out of hatred, aversion, and pride, he changes his posture and turns his shoulder.

He stated: man's state is strange: when We bestow our favor upon him, he acts arrogantly and rebels against Us; but when We catch him in some adversity as a punishment for his deeds, he becomes completely disappointed and despairing. Whereas the correct conduct for him was to remain grateful for Our favor and show patience in adversity. The meaning is: this is the state of your opponents. We have blessed them with Our favor, so in their pride, they are demanding the punishment from you; but if We catch them, all their pride will vanish, and they will become the very picture of despair and disappointment.

'Say, 'Each works according to his manner, but your Lord is most knowing of who is best guided in way.'

Although 'each' (kull) is indefinite, in some contexts—as we have clarified—it stands as a definite noun; meaning, it refers to those groups or persons who have been mentioned above.

Instruction to Leave the Matter to Allah: 'Manner' (shakilah) means path or way. The meaning is: tell them that if you are not ready to listen to my word, you will continue on your path, and I will remain firm on my call. Your Lord knows best who is on the straight path—you, in your own assumption, or I and my companions. The circumstances to come will show who reaches the destination. This verse is virtually a verse of delegation (tafweez)—directing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to leave their case to Allah and remain firm on his stand of truth.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 85 to 111: Replies to the Opponents' Objections: In the following, replies are given to those objections which the opponents raised against the Qur'an and the Prophet's messengership. Along with this, reference is also made to those demands which they proposed as a condition for their faith. Quoting these demands, He has replied to them, and comforted the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that those who possess the light of knowledge are believing in this Book; as for those who are demanding various miracles, leave them to their state, for they are not going to believe. Read the verses:

'And they ask you, [O Muhammad], about the soul [i.e., the revelation]. Say, 'The soul is of the affair [i.e., command] of my Lord. And mankind has not been given of knowledge except a little.'

The Meaning of 'Soul' (Rooh): 'Soul' here refers to divine revelation (wahy). In using the word 'soul' for revelation, there is an allusion that just as physical life is by the soul, spiritual, intellectual, and heart's life is by divine revelation. This reality was explained by Jesus: 'Man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceeds from the mouth of God.' The Qur'an has been termed as 'soul' in several places; for example: 'He sends down the angels with the soul of His command upon whom He wills of His servants, [saying], 'Warn that there is no deity except Me; so fear Me.' (An-Nahl: 2). 'He places the soul of His command upon whom He wills of His servants to warn of the Day of Meeting' (Ghafir: 15). 'And thus We have revealed to you an inspiration [i.e., soul] of Our command.' (Ash-Shura: 52).

Reply to the Question Regarding the Soul: The disbelievers asked about this soul, and their question was not for the sake of research, but was out of objection and mockery. That is, they meant to ask: what is this soul which you claim descends upon you? Explain its reality to us. In response, He stated: tell them that the soul is of the command of my Lord, and you have been given only a little of knowledge. That is, it is not necessary that you understand all the works of this universe and its Creator. This soul is understood only by those who have experienced it; if someone has never had a liver pain, how can he be shown or made to understand this pain?

In making this question, their minds also harbored this point: why does this soul descend only upon you, and not upon us? Facing this, the previous verses used the words 'upon whom He wills'—meaning, not every ambitious person is worthy of this honor, but Allah selects for it whom He wills.

'Of His command' (min amrihi) or 'of Our command' (min amrina) is to show that this is of the divine matters, the actual reality of which is known to God alone; not everyone can reach its reality.

Commentary on Verses 86 and 87: 'And if We willed, We could surely, do away with that which We revealed to you. Then you would not find for yourself concerning it an advocate against Us. Except [We have left it] as a mercy from your Lord. Indeed, His favor upon you has ever been great.'

The State of Revelation is a Divine Control: Although the address in this verse is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the point spoken is intended to be heard by those whose question has been quoted above. He stated: this state and quality of revelation is entirely an involuntary state for you, in which your choice, will, desire, or effort has no share. Neither can you bring it by your will, nor can you stop it by your will. To the extent that if We snatch this revelation which We have revealed to you, there is no power that can restore it to you. This is purely Our divine control by which you receive this, and is solely Our grace with which We have blessed you, and there is no doubt that this is a highly great favor of your Lord upon you.

'Say, 'If mankind and the jinn gathered in order to produce the like of this Quran, they could not produce the like of it, even if they were to each other assistants.'

Obviously, when even the Prophet's own desire and effort has no share in bringing this revelation, and he too is unable to present such a book by his choice and will—though it has manifested through him—what can be said of others? What power do others have to present the like of this Qur'an? Humans apart, even if humans and jinn combine and exert all their power to present such a book, they cannot do so. This challenge of the Qur'an has been present for about fourteen hundred years, and history bears witness that no one from among Arabs or non-Arabs had the courage to accept it, and if anyone attempted to copy it, he could not save himself from becoming a laughingstock.

Completion of Proof through the Qur'an: 'And We have certainly diversified for the people in this Quran from every [kind of] description, but most of the people refused [anything] except disbelief.'

'Tasreef' here means to explain a reality in various styles and diverse ways, and 'darb-e-mathal' means to speak words of wisdom and knowledge. This idiom is well known in Arabic. The line of a poet is famous:

'O Badr, the words of wisdom are spoken only for the wise.'

The meaning is: after the revelation of this miraculous Book—in which We have explained every word of wisdom in diverse styles and ways—these people have no excuse left that no guidance from God came to them. This Book has fully established the proof against them. If, after this, most of the people are still obstinate in their denial, it is their own misfortune.

Commentary on Verses 90 to 93: 

The Opponents' Demands for Confirming the Messengership: 'And they say, 'We will not believe you until you cause to gush forth for us from the earth a spring. Or [until] you have a garden of date palms and grapes and cause rivers to gush forth within them in force [and abundance]. Or you cause the heaven to fall upon us in fragments, as you have claimed, or you bring Allah and the angels before [us] face to face. Or you have a house of gold or you ascend into the sky. And we will not believe in your ascension until you bring down to us a book we may read.' Say, 'Exalted is my Lord! Was I [any] more than a human messenger?'

When 'imaan' (faith) is joined with the preposition 'li' (to), it does not carry the meaning of bringing faith, but means merely to accept or believe a statement. Now, those demands of the opponents are quoted which they proposed as a condition for confirming the Prophet's messengership. Those demands are:

1. That you cause a spring to gush forth from the earth before our eyes.

2. Or that you have a garden of date palms and grapes, and you cause many rivers to flow through its midst.

3. Or that you cause the heaven to fall upon us in fragments, as you claim, or you bring Allah and the angels face to face. 'Raituhu qabeela' means: 'I saw him face to face and directly.'

4. Or that you have a house of gold, or you ascend into the sky before our eyes; and even then, we will not believe your ascension until you bring down a book from there which we can read.

Reply to the Demands: In response to all these demands, He stated: 'Say, 'Exalted is my Lord! Was I [any] more than a human messenger?'—Tell them: 'My Lord is free from every partner; I am only a human and a messenger.' That is, I have not claimed divinity or partnership in divinity that you make such demands of me; my Lord is free, high, and above all partners, and I am only a human and a messenger of God. As a messenger, my duty is solely to convey God's message to you. I do not possess the power to perform any of these tasks.

The Wisdom of a Human Being Being a Prophet for Humans: 'And what prevented the people from believing when guidance came to them except that they said, 'Has Allah sent a human messenger?'

'Al-Huda' (the Guidance) refers to Allah's clear guidance with all its proofs and arguments. The meaning is: as far as Allah's guidance is concerned, it has come before them in a highly clear form, with its irrefutable proofs. Now, if they are presenting anything as an excuse for not believing, it is only this point: 'Has Allah sent a human messenger?!' That is, as far as proofs are concerned, there remains no room to deny them; but their pride and arrogance is preventing them from accepting a human as their messenger.

'Say, 'If there were upon the earth angels walking securely, We would have sent down to them from the heaven an angel as a messenger.' That is, if they assume that an angel, instead of a human, should have come to them as a messenger, tell them: if angels lived and walked securely on the earth instead of humans, then indeed, if We sent a messenger to them, We would have sent down an angel from the heaven; but when humans live on the earth, what is the meaning of sending an angel as a messenger to humans? A messenger comes so that his life may stand as an example and model for people. How can an angel's life stand as an example and model for humans?

With the word 'angels' (mala'ikah), the condition 'walking securely' (yamshoona mutma'inneen) points to the reality that angels do come and go on the earth, but they do so under God's command to implement His will. They do not exist here as permanent residents.

Instruction to Leave the Case to Allah: 'Say, 'Sufficient is Allah as a witness between me and you. Indeed, He is ever, of His servants, Acquainted and Seeing.' Now, after fully establishing the proof, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is directed to leave their case to Allah, telling them: 'Allah is sufficient as a witness between me and you as to whether the lack of clarity in proofs is preventing you from believing, or your stubbornness and ego.' Allah knows well the open and hidden states of His servants. He alone will now decide whether the truth of my messengership had not become clear to you, or you remained blind, deaf, and dumb despite seeing, hearing, and understanding everything.

The Divine Sunnah Regarding Guidance and Misguidance: 'And whoever Allah guides - he is the [rightly] guided; and whoever He sends astray - you will never find for them protectors besides Him. And We will gather them on the Day of Resurrection [fallen] on their faces - blind, dumb and deaf. Their refuge is Hell; every time it subsides We increase for them a blazing fire.' This explains Allah's sunnah regarding guidance and misguidance—showing that He alone guides whom He wills, and whom He leaves astray, no one can guide. The basis on which this divine sunnah is established is pointed to by the words: 'And We will gather them on the Day of Resurrection [fallen] on their faces - blind, dumb and deaf'—showing that those who do not use their eyes to see Allah's signs, their tongues to bear witness to the truth, and their ears to hear the words of Allah and His Messenger, do not receive Allah's guidance. Since their state is such, they will not receive guidance, and as a punishment for undervaluing these blessings, We will gather them on the Day of Resurrection, dragging them on their faces, in a state of being blind, dumb, and deaf. After the verb 'nahshuruhum' (We will gather them), the preposition 'ala' (on) is proof that they will be dragged on their faces. In another place, the words: 'The Day they are dragged into the Fire on their faces' have occurred, clarifying this theme. Those faces which Allah adorned with the capabilities of hearing, sight, and speech, yet they remained blind, deaf, and dumb, indeed deserve to be dragged on such a blazing fire whose flame never subsides.

He stated: 'That is their recompense because they disbelieved in Our verses and said, 'When we are bones and crumbled particles, will we truly be resurrected as a new creation?' This punishment will be meted out to them because—despite seeing the signs of Our power and resurrection at every step—they remained obstinate in denying the Resurrection, asking mockingly: 'When we are bones and crumbled particles, will we indeed be resurrected as a new creation?!' That is, in their assumption, this is completely impossible. He stated: We will make this very impossible a physical reality and make them taste its taste.

The Resurrection has an Appointed Time: 'Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth, is able to create the like of them? And He has appointed for them a term, about which there is no doubt. But the wrongdoers refuse [anything] except disbelief.' That is, why do they find so much impossibility in resurrection? Have they not considered that the God Who is powerful over creating the heavens and the earth, why would He be unable to create them again? Is creating them more difficult than creating the heavens and the earth? 'And He has appointed for them a term, about which there is no doubt'—as to the question of why the Resurrection does not happen immediately, they should not make haste; God has appointed a time for it, in the coming of which there is no doubt. When that time comes, that Promised Day will also appear. 'But the wrongdoers refuse [anything] except disbelief'—meaning, despite such clear proofs, these people who wrong their own selves are obstinate in denying it instead of preparing for it.

A Blow to the Arrogant Mindset of the Disbelievers: 'Say, 'If you possessed the depositories of the mercy of my Lord, then you would withhold out of fear of spending.' And ever is man stingy.'

We have stated above that in all this rejection, the arrogant mindset of the leaders of the Quraysh played a major part: they assumed that when all worldly blessings had been given to them, if God were to elevate anyone to the office of prophethood, He would have elevated one of them, so why did He bypass them to hand over this office to this poor man? Addressing this arrogant mindset of theirs, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was directed to tell them: 'If you were the owners of the depositories of my Lord's mercy, you would have indeed monopolized it, and out of fear of its being exhausted, you would have given nothing to anyone; but my Lord Himself is the Owner of His depositories of favor and mercy. He handed over to you those worthless pebbles of which He found you worthy, and despite your and the Jews' aversion, He chose me for this great favor.' In 'And ever is man stingy', the word 'man' (insan), though general, points to these very people—showing that if they are stingy, and there is no room in their hearts for anyone else, why have they assumed God to be like themselves?

Answer to the Demand for Miracles: 'And We had certainly given Moses nine clear signs, so ask the Children of Israel when he came to them and Pharaoh said to him, 'Indeed, I think, O Moses, that you are affected by magic.'

In verses 90 to 93 above, those miracles were mentioned which the Quraysh demanded. This is the answer to that demand—showing that the path of faith and guidance is not obtained through miracles. We gave Moses nine clear miracles upon Pharaoh's demand, but Pharaoh, after seeing all these miracles, said: 'O Moses, I think you are a man affected by magic. Someone has cast a spell on you, under the influence of which you are speaking such absurd things before me.' The meaning is: similarly, if you are shown miracles upon your demand, you too will find some excuse.

The Example of Moses: 'so ask the Children of Israel' contains a subtle allusion. That is, in this stage, the Children of Israel had joined the campaign of opposing Islam, and the Quraysh made these demands for miracles mostly at their instigation. They taught the Quraysh: 'Our Prophet showed such and such miracles, so if this person claims to be a prophet, he should also show similar miracles.' Due to this action of theirs, the Qur'an presented them as witnesses: 'Their Prophet indeed showed miracles, but ask them what was the result of showing all those miracles? If the result was that Pharaoh and his hosts drowned, why do they show this path to the Quraysh?'

Moses's Reply to Pharaoh: '[Moses] said, 'You have already known that none has sent down these [signs] except the Lord of the heavens and the earth as evidence. And indeed, I think, O Pharaoh, that you are destroyed.'

'Mathboora' means destroyed. Moses replied to Pharaoh in the same tone. He said: 'You know well that these miracles cannot be the work of magic. Their nature itself bears witness that none can send them down except the Lord of the heavens and the earth, and He has sent them to open your eyes. If your eyes have not opened even after seeing them, God's proof has been fully established against you, and I think, O Pharaoh, that the hour of your destruction has drawn near.'

'So he intended to drive them from the land, but We drowned him and those with him all together.' Consequently, after this, he exerted all his power to uproot the steps of Moses and his companions from the land of Egypt, and the punishment he received was that God drowned him and all his hosts.

Favor Upon the Children of Israel After Drowning Pharaoh: 'And We said after him [i.e., Pharaoh] to the Children of Israel, 'Dwell in the land, and when there comes the promise of the Hereafter, We will bring you forth in [one] gathering.' After drowning Pharaoh, this was a favor upon the Children of Israel: Allah made them dwell in the Holy Land. 'The land' (Al-Ard) here refers to the Holy Land which was promised to the Children of Israel. While fulfilling this promise, Allah also reminded them of the promise of the Hereafter—directing them not to forget the Hereafter in the joys of this success: 'Just as We have gathered you here according to Our promise, similarly We will gather you for accountability on the Day of Resurrection according to Our promise.' But the Children of Israel completely forgot this reminder of Allah.

The Prophet's Responsibility in the Matter of Propagation: 'And with the truth We have sent it down, and with the truth it has descended. And We have not sent you, [O Muhammad], except as a bringer of good tidings and a warner.'

Keep in view that this discussion began with the mention of revelation and the Qur'an, and then other related issues became under discussion. Now, as these issues conclude, the original issue is taken up again. He stated: We have revealed this Qur'an with the truth, and it has descended with the truth. No falsehood can mix with it from before or from behind. If they make such an unadulterated truth the target of various doubts, you hold no responsibility for the faith and guidance of such stubborn people. You are only a bringer of good tidings and a warner. Perform your duty of warning and giving glad tidings, and leave them to their state. If they do not listen to your word, they will face the end.

The Wisdom Behind the Gradual Revelation of the Qur'an: 'And [it is] a Quran which We have divided [into parts] that you might recite it to the people at intervals. And We have sent it down progressively.' That is, this Qur'an which is being revealed in parts is not because—as they assume—you think according to circumstances and recite as much as you can prepare, but We are revealing it to you in parts so that you may recite it to the people gradually, so that it becomes a part of their thought and action. If they assume that if it were a divine Book, it would have been revealed completely at once because God is not in need of preparation, this assumption is also incorrect. God is indeed independent of any preparation; He could have revealed the entire Book at once, but He had regard for His servants' need and their circumstances, and He has revealed it with gradualness and care. 'Tanzeel' (progressive revelation) means to reveal a thing with gradualness and care.

The Conduct of the Righteous Among the People of the Book: 'Say, 'Believe in it or do not believe. Indeed, those who were given knowledge before it - when it is recited to them, they fall upon their faces in prostration, And they say, 'Exalted is our Lord! Indeed, the promise of our Lord has been fulfilled.' And they fall upon their faces weeping, and it increases them in humble submission.'

'Those who were given knowledge before it' refers to the good among the People of the Book. Just as there were wicked and corrupt among the People of the Book who expended all their power in opposing the Qur'an, there was also a group of righteous ones who, in the light of the prophecies in their scriptures, were waiting for a promised messenger and book. This group saw in the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the scripture he brought the reflections of those prophecies for whose appearance they were waiting. Consequently, their state was such that when the Qur'an was recited to them, they fell prostrate, thanking God from their heart and soul for the fulfillment of His promise. This refers to this blessed group. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is directed: tell the deniers of the Quraysh and the Children of Israel: 'Believe in it or do not believe; it is sufficient for my satisfaction that a group of the people of knowledge exists who, hearing this Qur'an, fall prostrate, and seeing this fulfillment of God's promise, are overcome by tears of joy, gratitude, and humble submission.' The aspect of gratitude and joy in it is obvious; the aspect of increase in humble submission in it is that this has given them a fresh reminder of the promise of the Hereafter—showing that the Lord Who fulfilled this promise of His will surely fulfill His promise of the Hereafter too.

An Excuse to Object to the Qur'an: 'Say, 'Call upon Allah or call upon the Most Merciful [i.e., Al-Rahman]. Whichever [name] you call - to Him belong the best names.' And do not recite [too] loudly in your prayer or [too] quietly but seek between that a [moderate] way.'

When suspicion and bad opinion against something take root, suspicion begins to arise even regarding those of its things in which there is no room for any doubt. The Arabs in the days of ignorance used both the names 'Allah' and 'Rahman' for God. Both these names are found in the literature of the days of ignorance. However, the name 'Rahman' was more well known among the People of the Book. The clever ones among the Arabs made this name also an excuse to object to the Qur'an. They raised this point: 'In preparing this book, some of the People of the Book help him.' Their statement: 'and another people helped him at it' points to this group. Later, when they saw the name 'Rahman' in the Qur'an, they presented it in support of their assumption—claiming: 'Look, he uses the name 'Rahman' frequently in his discourse, which is proof of his connection with the People of the Book,' and from this they concluded that this is a conspiracy to impose the religion and traditions of the People of the Book upon our religion and traditions. The Qur'an replied: call Him by the name of 'Allah' or call Him by the name of 'Rahman', whichever name you use, all beautiful names belong to Him. That is, prejudice regarding a mere name must not prevent the acceptance of truth. God has other names too, and He can be called by any name befitting His majesty.

Teaching Dignity and Sobriety in Prayer: 'And do not recite [too] loudly...'—it appears that a similar objection was raised by these people against the Islamic method of prayer. In the polytheists' method of worship, whistling and clapping of hands were of great importance. Contrary to this, prayer in Islam is the perfect manifestation of sobriety and dignity. It is possible that they linked this dignity of prayer also with the People of the Book and made it an excuse for objection. In this matter, the instruction was given: neither pray too loudly nor too quietly, but choose a moderate path between them. This points to the fact that you are a moderate nation (Ummah-e-Wast), so your prayers and supplications must also reflect this moderate character.

Declaration of Allah's Absolute Sovereignty: 'And say, 'Praise to Allah, who has not taken a son and has had no partner in [His] kingdom and has no need of a protector out of weakness; and glorify Him with [great] glorification.'

At the end of the Surah, you are directed to praise and glorify God, declaring His absolute sovereignty—meaning, let these people wander in whatever valley they want, but you declare: 'The praise belongs to Allah Who has not taken a son, Who has no partner in His kingdom, and Who never suffers any weakness that He needs any supporter or protector.' 'and has no need of a protector out of weakness' points to the reality that a supporter or protector is needed by one who fears facing some weakness. When God's majesty is above every trace of weakness, why does He need any supporter or protector? 'and glorify Him with [great] glorification'—meaning, declare and express all His sublime attributes with great care, contrary to what these polytheists say.

With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. And our last call is that all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the worlds.
